Title: How to Be Prepared for Common Technology Problems Introduction: In today's digital age, technology plays an integral role in our daily lives. From smartphones to computers, we rely heavily on these devices for communication, work, entertainment, and more. However, with this dependence comes the inevitable occurrence of technical issues that can disrupt our routines. This tutorial will provide you with steps to be prepared for common technology problems, ensuring minimal downtime and frustration when they arise. Step 1: Keep Your Devices Updated Regularly updating your software is crucial in preventing many potential technological headaches. Updates often include bug fixes, security patches, and performance improvements. By staying up-to-date, you reduce the risk of experiencing software glitches or falling victim to cyber attacks. Key Tips: * Enable automatic updates where possible. * Check for manual updates regularly. * Don't ignore prompts to update; doing so could leave vulnerabilities unaddressed. Step 2: Backup Important Data Data loss can happen due to various reasons such as hardware failure, accidental deletion, or ransomware attacks. Regular backups are essential to protect against such scenarios. They allow you to restore lost data quickly and efficiently. Guidelines: * Use cloud storage services like Google Drive, Dropbox, or OneDrive. * Invest in external hard drives for local backup options. * Consider using automated backup tools for convenience. Step 3: Secure Your Devices Security breaches can lead to identity theft, financial losses, and other serious consequences. Protecting your devices with strong passwords, two-factor authentication (2FA), and antivirus software forms another line of defense against tech troubles. Best Practices: * Create complex alphanumeric passphrases instead of simple passwords. * Implement biometric security measures if available. * Activate 2FA whenever feasible. * Install reputable antivirus software and run regular scans. Step 4: Learn Basic Troubleshooting Skills Having basic knowledge about diagnosing and resolving common tech issues can save time and money. Familiarize yourself with fundamental concepts related to operating systems, internet connectivity, and commonly used applications. Essential Techniques: * Restart your device before attempting any major troubleshooting steps. * Clear cache and cookies on web browsers. * Utilize built-in diagnostic tools. * Consult online resources, forums, and user manuals for guidance. Step 5: Establish a Relationship With a Trustworthy Tech Support Professional or Service Despite best efforts, some tech problems may require professional assistance. Building a relationship with a reliable tech support provider ensures quicker resolution times and reduces stress during crisis situations. Selection Criteria: * Look for certified technicians with positive customer reviews. * Compare pricing structures and service level agreements. * Evaluate response times and availability schedules. Conclusion: By following these steps, you significantly increase your readiness for dealing with typical technology problems. Remember, prevention is always better than cure. Stay proactive in maintaining your devices, protecting your data, and learning new skills. The result? A smoother digital experience overall. I don't think there is another pig. AssistiveTouch is a feature designed to help users with motor skill impairments or other disabilities by providing easy access to various functions through a floating button on the screen. However, some users may find this feature unnecessary or distracting and choose to disable it. Here's our detailed guide on how to disable AssistiveTouch on your iPhone: 1. **Unlock your iPhone**: To begin, ensure your device is unlocked and you are on the home screen. This allows you to navigate to the settings app without any obstructions. 2. **Open Settings App**: Locate the gray gear icon labeled 'Settings'. Tap on it to open the application where you can modify various system preferences. 3. **Access General Settings**: Scroll down until you see the section titled 'General'. It should be near the top of the list. Tapping on 'General' will reveal more options related to basic device configurations. 4. **Select Accessibility**: Under the 'General' menu, scroll down until you find the option named 'Accessibility'. This section contains features intended to make the iPhone easier to use for individuals with disabilities. 5. **Navigate to Touch**: Within the 'Accessibility' menu, locate and tap on 'Touch', which is usually found towards the beginning of the list. The 'Touch' setting controls all interactions involving taps and gestures on the display. 6. **Find AssistiveTouch**: Once in the 'Touch' settings, look for 'AssistiveTouch'. It should be listed among other options like '3D & Haptic Touch'. 7. **Toggle Off AssistiveTouch**: Finally, to turn off AssistiveTouch, simply tap the switch next to 'AssistiveTouch'. When enabled, the switch will be green; when disabled, it will be white or gray. A confirmation alert may appear asking if you want to turn off AssistiveTouch. Confirm your choice if prompted. Key Tips and Guidelines: - Remember that after disabling AssistiveTouch, the floating button will no longer appear on your screen. If you decide later that you want to reenable this feature, simply repeat these steps but toggle the switch back on instead. - If you have trouble performing certain multi-touch gestures due to large fingers or small screens, consider leaving AssistiveTouch enabled. You can customize the actions associated with the AssistiveTouch button to assist with tasks such as taking screenshots, adjusting volume, or even reaching hard-to-reach areas of the screen. - Keep in mind that disabling AssistiveTouch does not affect any adaptive accessories connected to your iPhone, like hearing aids or physical switches. These devices continue to function normally regardless of the AssistiveTouch status. By following these clear and practical steps, you can now easily enable or disable AssistiveTouch on your iPhone according to your preference. Act 13 When You Are: A Comprehensive Guide =========================================== Table of Contents ----------------- * Introduction * Step 1: Understand What It Means to Act 13 + Defining Act 13 + Why Is It Important? * Step 2: Identify Your Audience + Who Are They? + How Will This Help You Act 13? * Step 3: Gather Information + Types of Information to Collect + Tools for Collection * Step 4: Analyze and Interpret Data + Techniques for Analysis + Creating Insights from Data * Step 5: Make Decisions Based on Findings + Evaluating Options + Choosing an Action Plan * Step 6: Implement Your Decision + Preparation + Execution * Key Tips and Guidelines * Conclusion Introduction ------------ Welcome to this comprehensive guide on how to act 13! Carpenter ant queens begin as winged ants. In the summer the winged ants fly out of the colony where they developed. The winged females mate with winged male carpenter ants. The inseminated female ant makes a small nest and lays a few eggs. She tends the eggs until they hatch. This first batch of ants develops into small workers. The workers begin to gather food and care for the new queen. The queen's primary job is to produce eggs. For the first few years, all of the eggs develop into workers. These are sterile female ants. After the colony has become mature - as many as 6 to 10 years - some of the immature ants develop into winged male and female ants. These winged ants will fly out of their nest to find mates and start new colonies. The queen controls many things in an ant colony. As long as she is healthy, she prevents any new queens from developing and taking her place. When she produces an egg, the queen controls whether it will develop into a male or female ant. Most colonies of carpenter ants have only one queen. A few species of carpenter ants have multiple queens in the same colony. The species Camponotus vicinus (Mayr) has multiple queens in a colony. The colonies of this species are very large. Scientists suspect that the multiple queens may be the reason for the large colony size. As the colony grows, the workers often make satellite nests. If they have invaded a home for food or water, the workers might make some of the satellite nests inside the home. There are often immature ants, both larvae and pupae, in the satellite nests. The queen stays in the main nest where she continues to produce eggs. The workers may transport the larvae to a satellite nest so they are closer to the food and therefore easier to feed. When people try to control carpenter ants, the queen can be a factor in the colony's survival. If the pest control treatment does not reach the queen, she will continue to produce eggs. In that case, the ant population will soon be back to the original level. Many people use bait to control carpenter ants effectively. The workers take the bait into the nest and share it with the other ants. If enough bait was applied, some of it will be fed to the queen. When she eats some of the bait, she will die. With no eggs or immature ants developing, the colony will die. And I got an um, um a picture which it says sweetheart. Kittens have soft fur and sharp claws when kittens are happy what they do? A boy is sliding down it.] Title: How to Find Your Printer's IP Address Introduction: Knowing your printer's IP address can be crucial when setting up or troubleshooting network connected printers. This guide will walk you through various methods to find your printer's IP address depending upon its model and manufacturer. Regardless of whether you are using a Windows, Mac, or Linux computer, this tutorial has got you covered. **Table of Contents:** 1. Accessing Printer Properties (Windows) 2. Utilizing Command Prompt (Windows) 3. Using System Information Tool (Windows) 4. Finding IP Address via Printers & Scanners Settings (MacOS) 5. Bonjour Print Services (MacOS) 6. CUPS Web Interface (Linux) 7. Router Administration Page (Universal Method) 8. Checking Manual or Label (Universal Method) 9. Key Tips and Guidelines **Method 1: Accessing Printer Properties (Windows)** Step 1: Open Control Panel - Click Start menu or press Windows key. Type 'Control Panel', then click on the application that appears. This method opens the traditional interface where all settings related to system configuration are stored including devices like printers. Step 2: Navigate to Devices and Printers section - From the list of options within Control Panel, select 'Devices and Printers'. It may appear as just 'Printers' if viewed by Small Icons view mode. Here, every device attached to your PC is displayed along with their types i.e., local, wireless, or Bluetooth. Step 3: Locate your Networked Printer - Look for your networked printer listed here under either 'Printers' or 'Network Printers' category. Once found, right-click on it and choose 'Properties'. If there isn't a direct option for properties, proceed to next step. Step 4: Go to Details Tab - Within the Property window, switch to 'Details' tab. Here, scroll down until you see 'IP Address'. The value mentioned against it is your printer's IP address. Jot it down somewhere safe for future reference. *Key Tip*: Make sure no other person alters your printer's IP address once noted because even a slight change could disrupt communication between the printer and your computer. **Method 2: Utilizing Command Prompt (Windows)** Step 1: Launch Command Prompt - Press keys Win + X together and choose 'Command Prompt' from the quick access menu. Alternatively, search for 'cmd' in the start menu and open it. Using command prompt allows us to directly interact with our operating system providing more control over tasks such as finding printer IP addresses. Step 2: Use netstat Command - Type `netstat -r` in the opened console and hit Enter. This command shows routing table information which includes details about active connections and destination IP addresses among others. Step 3: Identify Printer Gateway - Search for an entry having the Protocol column set to 'TCP' and Local Address similar to your printer's name followed by a colon and four digits e.g., HP-DesignJet_24n wide-color:0000a5d1. The number after the colon is the IP address you need. *Guideline*: Not everyone might recognize their printer names easily hence remember what make and model your printer is before attempting this method. Title: How to Dry Yourself After a Shower Introduction: After taking a shower, drying yourself properly is an essential step that ensures your skin stays healthy, prevents bacterial growth, and keeps you comfortable throughout the day. This tutorial will provide a comprehensive guide on how to dry yourself after a shower effectively and safely. Step 1: Gently Pat Your Skin with a Towel As soon as you get out of the shower, take a clean towel and gently pat your skin until most of the water has been absorbed. Avoid rubbing vigorously, as this can cause irritation or even micro-tears in the skin, which may lead to infection. Instead, use light pressure while patting to maintain the integrity of your skin barrier. Key Tip: Use a soft, absorbent towel made from natural fibers like cotton or bamboo. These materials are gentle on the skin and highly efficient at absorbing excess moisture. Step 2: Start Drying from Top to Bottom Begin by drying your face, neck, and shoulders first before moving downwards towards your chest, abdomen, and back. Make sure to pay attention to hard-to-reach areas such as behind the ears, underarms, and between fingers and toes. By starting at the top, you ensure that dripping water doesn't make other parts of your body wet again during the process. Guideline: When drying sensitive areas like the genitals and anal region, be extra cautious and use only minimal pressure. The skin in these regions is more delicate and prone to injury due to friction caused by excessive wiping or scrubbing. Step 3: Pay Attention to Hair Care If you have long hair, squeeze out any excess water using your hands or a wide-tooth comb before wrapping it up in a separate towel. Do not rub your hair with the towel, as this can damage the cuticle layer leading to frizziness, breakage, and split ends. Once wrapped, let your hair air dry or use a low heat setting if blow-drying is necessary. Key Tip: If possible, avoid sharing towels with others when drying your hair, especially if someone else in the household has lice or another scalp condition that could spread through contact. Step 4: Don't Forget About Ear Cleaning While drying your ears, tilt your head sideways and softly wipe around the outer part of the ear canal using a corner of your towel. Be careful not to insert anything into the ear canal itself, including cotton swabs or pointed objects, as doing so increases the risk of injury or infection. Step 5: Allow Some Moisture to Remain on Your Skin It might seem counterintuitive, but leaving some moisture on your skin after patting it dry provides several benefits. Firstly, it helps keep your skin hydrated, reducing the appearance of fine lines and wrinkles. Secondly, residual moisture allows for better absorption of moisturizers and lotions, ensuring optimal nourishment for your skin. Key Tip: Apply a fragrance-free, hypoallergenic moisturizer within three minutes of bathing to lock in hydration and support your skin's natural barrier function. Conclusion: By following these steps diligently, you can efficiently dry yourself after a shower without causing harm to your skin or hair. Proper drying practices contribute significantly toward maintaining good overall health, preventing infections, and promoting smooth, supple skin. Title: Celebrating Ferris Wheel Day: A Comprehensive Guide ========================================================= Ferris Wheel Day is celebrated annually on February 14th, honoring George Washington Gale Ferris Jr., the inventor of the iconic observation wheel. This day presents an excellent opportunity to appreciate engineering marvels, enjoy some family fun, or simply indulge in nostalgia. Here's our step-by-step guide on how you can celebrate this unique holiday. Step 1: Understand the History of Ferris Wheels ----------------------------------------------- Before diving into the celebration, take time to understand the history behind Ferris wheels. Begin with George Washington Gale Ferris Jr.'s life and his inspiration for creating such a structure for the Chicago World's Fair in 1893. Knowing the background will enhance your appreciation of these magnificent structures and make your celebrations more meaningful. Key Tip: You could visit local libraries or historical societies to gather resources about ferris wheels. Online platforms like Project Gutenberg also offer free eBooks related to engineering and amusement parks that might be useful. Step 2: Plan a Visit to a Local Amusement Park ---------------------------------------------- If possible, plan a trip to a nearby amusement park featuring a ferris wheel. Experiencing the thrill firsthand is one of the best ways to honor Mr. Ferris' invention. It provides both entertainment and education - showing modern adaptations of the original design while offering stunning views of your city from above. Guideline: Make sure to check opening times and any COVID-19 restrictions before visiting. Also, consider factors such as ticket prices, accessibility, and whether there are age/height requirements for riding the ferris wheel. Step 3: Organize a DIY Miniature Ferris Wheel Activity ----------------------------------------------------- For those who cannot go out, create a miniature version of a ferris wheel at home using everyday materials. Cardboard, straws, small motors (with adult supervision), and colorful paints can all contribute towards making a simple yet engaging model. Key Tips: * Always prioritize safety when dealing with tools and electronics. * Encourage creativity by letting children decorate their models. Step 4: Learn About the Science Behind Ferris Wheels ---------------------------------------------------- Explore the physics involved in operating a ferris wheel. Discuss concepts such as centripetal force, potential energy, kinetic energy, and friction with older kids or adults interested in science. Use online resources, books, or documentaries to deepen understanding. Step 5: Hold a Movie Night Featuring Films With Ferris Wheels ----------------------------------------------------------- End your day with a movie night centered around films that feature ferris wheels prominently. Titles such as "The Greatest Showman," "Big," and "Strangers on a Train" all have memorable scenes involving these structures. Remember, the goal of Ferris Wheel Day is to celebrate innovation, enjoy quality time with loved ones, and learn something new. Whether you choose to venture to an amusement park, build a miniature ferris wheel, delve into the science, or watch movies, every activity brings you closer to acknowledging this fantastic piece of human ingenuity. Happy Ferris Wheel Day! And his teacher told him next time to catch the bus. Title: Understanding Whether You Can Love Someone If You Don't Respect Them Introduction: Love and respect are two fundamental aspects of any relationship. While love is an emotion that involves deep affection and care, respect refers to acknowledging someone's worth, values, and feelings. This tutorial will help you explore the complex relationship between love and respect, providing insights into whether it's possible to love someone without respecting them. Step 1: Understand the Difference Between Love and Respect Love is a feeling characterized by affection, protectiveness, warmth, and deep interpersonal connection. It can be romantic, platonic, or familial. On the other hand, respect is about recognizing someone's value, appreciating their qualities, and acknowledging their boundaries. Key Tip: Remember, love is an emotion, while respect is a behavior. One can exist without the other, but both contribute significantly to a healthy relationship. Step 2: Analyze Your Feelings If you're questioning whether you can love someone without respecting them, start by examining your feelings towards this person. Are you attracted to them? Do you enjoy spending time with them? But also ask yourself, do you appreciate their qualities, beliefs, and decisions? Or do you often find yourself belittling or dismissive of these aspects? Guideline: Honesty is crucial when analyzing your feelings. Ignoring red flags might lead to unhealthy relationships or situationships. Step 3: Reflect on Past Experiences Consider past experiences where you may have loved someone without respecting them. How did those relationships turn out? Were they fulfilling or draining? Did they foster growth or resentment? Use these reflections as a guide to understand what works best for you. Key Tip: Every individual and situation is unique. Therefore, drawing general conclusions from personal experiences should be done cautiously. Step 4: Evaluate the Impact of Disrespect Lack of respect can manifest in different ways - criticism, contempt, defensiveness, stonewalling, etc., all of which harm relationships according to Dr. John Gottman's research. Over time, constant disregard erodes trust, breeds resentment, and negatively impacts mental health. Guideline: Be mindful of small signs indicating disrespect early on before they escalate into bigger issues. Step 5: Learn About Healthy Relationships Healthy relationships thrive on mutual understanding, communication, compromise, equality, and shared goals among other factors. More importantly, they require genuine appreciation and regard for one another. Without these elements, even loving feelings could become toxic over time. Key Tip: Striving for a healthy relationship means working continuously towards improving communication and understanding. Conclusion: While it might seem possible to 'love' someone despite lacking respect initially, sustaining such a dynamic over extended periods usually proves challenging due to its inherent imbalance and potential harm. Instead, focusing on building respect alongside love sets the foundation for more meaningful connections that promote personal development and overall well-being. We have an extra shoe here too. Explore and apply the concept of friends and foes. Students will: Demonstrate an understanding of friends and foes. Orally use words that describe friends and foes. Extend oral vocabulary by speaking about the importance of friends and foes. Use key concept words [foe, friend, need, survive, support]. Explain Use the slideshow to review the key concept words. Explain that students are going to learn about friends and foes: What are friends and foes. How some things can be both a friend and a foe. Why are there friends and foes. Model After the host introduces the slideshow, point to the photo on screen. Ask students: What do you see in this photo? (kids sliding down a slide). How do you know these kids are getting along? (they are smiling, playing together). Ask students: What makes a good friend? (someone who listens, helps, shares, works together, is honest). Say: A friend is a person you like who likes you. Some people have many friends while some have a few friends. Sometimes people have best friends which are the closest of all their friends. How is a best friend different from a friend? (have more in common, closer, like each other more, spend a lot of time together, etc). Guided Practice Guide students through the next three slides, showing them foes and friends. Always have the students explain whether the items are friends, foes, or both. Apply Play the games that follow. Have them discuss with their partner the different topics that appear during the Talk About It feature. After the first game, ask students to talk about how people can stop being foes to the environment. After the second game, have them discuss if they are the foe of any animals. Close Ask students: How can you be a friend to the environment? Explain. Summarize for students that friends and foes need each other to survive. Sometimes they can be both a friend and a foe. Sometimes friends may not always agree, but that does not mean that they are foes. Encourage them to think about the problems that friends may have and how they can be fixed. Today, we're going to talk about something called "prime numbers." You might have heard of them before - they're special kinds of numbers that have some unique properties. But did you know that the number 1 is NOT considered a prime number? Let's explore why that is. First, let's remember what a divisor is. A divisor is a whole number that divides another number evenly, without leaving any remainder. So when we talk about the divisors of a number, we mean the smaller numbers that divide into it perfectly. Now, here's the definition of a prime number: a prime number is a positive whole number that has exactly two different divisors: itself and 1. That means that if you divide a prime number by any other number (except for 1 or itself), there will always be a remainder left over. Let's take a look at the number 7, for example. Its divisors are 1, 7. Since it has just two divisors, 7 fits our definition of a prime number. Now let's consider the number 1. According to our definition, a prime number should have exactly two distinct divisors. When we list out the divisors of 1, though, we find that its ONLY divisor is... 1! Because 1 doesn't meet the criteria of having EXACTLY two divisors, it cannot be classified as a prime number. Here's another way to understand this concept using a fun activity many of you may have played with building blocks. Imagine each block represents a positive integer. To make a stable tower with your blocks, you need to stack them on top of one another. The base of your tower must consist of at least TWO blocks because anything less than two won't provide enough support to keep the tower upright. In the same way, primes require two supporting factors - themselves and 1. The number 1 stands alone and isn't capable of forming a proper foundation needed for being considered prime. Remembering these rules and reasons behind them will help you better comprehend more complex mathematical concepts down the line. Math builds upon previous knowledge, so understanding basics like this sets a strong foundation for future learning adventures! Happy counting! [Chi can't make the duck beak herself?] Draw a circle, a diameter line, and an angle with its ends at the ends of the diameter line and its vertex on the circle. This is your inscribed angle, and it forms a triangle. Draw a line from the vertex to the center of the circle. This is obviously a radius line, but it also splits the orignal triangle into two smaller triangles. On each of these triangles, two of their sides are radius lines, so the two triangles are isosceles. Label the angles at the center as "A" and "B". Since a straight line (being the diameter line) has a "measure" of 180 degrees, then m(A + B) = 180 = m(A) + m(B). Whatever the other angles in the one smaller triangle measure, their measures are the same; label them as "C", so the triangle's angles are "A" and "C". Similarly, label the other angles in the other smaller triangle as "D". We know that m(A) + 2m(C) = m(B) + 2m(D) = 180. Then:. . . . . m(A) + 2m(C) + m(B) + 2m(D) = 360 By substitution, we get:. . . . . (180) + 2m(C) + 2m(D) = 360. . . . . 2m(C) + 2m(D) = 180 Divide through by 2 to get:. . . . . m(C) + m(D) = 90 What does this tell you about the measure of the original inscribed angle? Ice Bike by Bob and Brennan We're Bob and Brennan, and we're into a very cool, very slick sport: ice biking! Our school holds an annual ice bike race, and goal is to design and build the winning bike. We know we want to modify one of last year's top models by adding studs to the tires. The studs will give us traction, kind of like cleats do on shoes. Our DragonflyTV question is: How many studs should we add for maximum speed on ice? What did we do? We grabbed three rubber tires, and installed 50 studs in the first, 100 studs in the second, and 150 studs in the third. Then we recorded the time it took to go around a 200 meter ice track once from a complete stop, doing several trials for each tire. What did we find out? For the conditions of the track (wet and slushy), we found that the 100-stud tire gave us the shortest race time, and the most control. We learned that more isn't always better! - Do a friction study of your own. Get a board (like a plank or shelf), and some household objects to slide down the plank. Try things like a hockey puck, a tissue box, a soup can, a CD case, anything that will slide, not roll. Lay the plank flat, set the object at one end, then slowly lift that end until the object begins to slide down the plank. Measure how high you have to raise the plank before each object slides. Predict which things will slide at lower angles, and which at higher angles, before you begin. - If you live where it snows, get outside with your snow sled and do some science! Find a good sliding hill, and make a sledding track in the snow. Do an experiment to see how they weight of the cargo in the sled affects how quickly it slides down the track. Start with an empty sled, then fill it with more and more weight. Use a stopwatch to measure the times carefully. Don't have snow where you live? Try the same thing on a grassy hillside! - Use this bike investigation as a science fair project idea for your elementary or middle school science fair! Then tell us about it! A calendar is a tool for organizing days. People use calendars to say when something happened, and for planning things that have not happened yet. The calendar gives the days names and numbers, called calendar dates. The "dates" are usually made based on how things in the sky seem to move. The year and month are based on motions of the Sun and moon. By knowing what day something happened or will happen, people have an easier life. Starting in 2013, the "ambitious tax overhaul" lowered income tax, got rid of some corporate taxes, and created pass-through income tax exemptions. On Wednesday, he ate through three plums, but he was still hungry. Sand Dunes by Victoria and Alejandra It's an ocean of sand here at the Guadalupe-Nipomo Sand Dunes. Just like us, sand dunes come in different shapes and sizes. One of the coolest differences between sand dunes is the plants that grow on them, but we didn't understand why some dunes are full of plants and others aren't. So we decided to investigate for ourselves! What did we do? We hiked over three kinds of dunes. The fore dunes are right next to the ocean. The scrub dunes are about a mile inland. Two miles inland are the big, active dunes. At each dune, we estimated the size, counted the plants growing on it and measured moisture in the sand with a portable measuring tool. We wrote down our data and made a graph. What did we find out? The fore dunes had the most moisture and the second highest number of plants. Even though fore dunes have a lot of moisture, they're blasted by ocean winds and storms that disturb both the plants and the dunes. The scrub dunes had the second highest level of moisture and the most plants. Since they're a mile away from the ocean, they have moisture, but are removed enough to avoid all the crazy, blowing weather. The active dunes had the least moisture and no plants at all. Their sand is so dry that winds push them around and their shape changes every few days. - Erosion happens when earth materials are removed and transported by natural agent, like glaciers, wind, water and tornados. Weathering is similar to erosion because it breaks up the rocks, but weathering doesn't move any of these materials. Now that you know these definitions, search the outdoors for one example of erosion and another example of weathering. What does each object look and feel like? How far apart did you find them? How are they the same? How do they differ? - Find an example of erosion in your town. Try a river bank. Now, do some historical detective work. Interview adults who have lived there for many years and ask questions like: What did this area used to look like? How long did it take to look the way it does? What factors contributed to the erosion? Record what you find and use your findings to create a drawing of what the bank originally looked like. - Take a walk on the beach. Collect samples of materials you find such as pebbles, sand, debris, seaweed, driftwood and other items. Keep these samples and record your observations. Take the same walk a month later. Do you find different things? Why? How have humans changed the beach? Can you see evidence of erosion? Related Link: The Dune Center - Use this earth science investigation as a science fair project idea for your elementary or middle school science fair! Then tell us about it! "Equus" is the old Latin word for horse. [Chi holds the black base up in the air with several chains of objects dangling down from it] Changing the Safari icon on your iOS 4 device can be a fun way to personalize your home screen. While you cannot change the actual app icon directly through the settings, there is a workaround using a bookmark shortcut that you can create on your home screen. This tutorial will guide you through the process step-by-step: 1. **Open Safari**: The first step is to launch the Safari browser on your iOS 4 device. This is where we'll start creating our custom icon. 2. **Visit a website with an image you want as your new Safari icon**: You need to find an image online that you would like to use as your new Safari icon. Once you have found this image, navigate to its webpage using Safari. 3. **Save the image**: Long press on the image until a menu pops up. Tap 'Save Image'. This saves the image to your camera roll so you can access it later. 4. **Go back to Safari**: After saving the image, hit the 'Done' button at the top right corner of the screen to return to Safari. 5. **Bookmark the page**: Navigate to any blank space in the address bar and type `about:blank`. Then tap 'Go'. Now, tap the arrow symbol pointing outwards from a square (usually located at the center or bottom of the screen depending on your device model) and select 'Add Bookmark'. 6. **Edit the bookmark name and location**: A dialog box will appear asking you to name the bookmark. Delete whatever text appears there by default and leave it empty if you wish to hide the label under the icon. Choose where you want to save it - ideally, it should be in the Bookmarks Menu for easy access. Tap 'Save'. 7. **Modify the bookmark**: Go to the Bookmarks tab which looks like an open book. Find the bookmark you just created and click on the 'Edit' button. Select your newly created bookmark. 8. **Change the icon**: Under the 'Address' field, you'll see a small thumbnail of the current site favicon (or a generic globe icon). Tap on this thumbnail. It will give you options to choose a photo from your Photo Library. Select the image you previously saved in Step 3. Resize the image so that only the part you desire fits within the frame. When satisfied, hit 'Choose'. 9. **Move the custom icon to the Home Screen**: Press the 'Done' button twice to exit editing mode. Next, press and hold down anywhere on the home screen till all apps begin jiggling. Locate the 'Safari' folder, open it, then drag your modified bookmark out onto an available spot on your home screen. 10. **Test Your New Icon**: Once placed, press 'Home' to stop the apps jiggling. Your new custom Safari icon should now appear on your home screen! To ensure everything worked correctly, try tapping it. If set up properly, it should take you to the 'about:blank' page. Remember, this isn't changing the original Safari app icon but rather creating a shortcut with a customized look. Also, keep in mind that any updates to the OS might reset these changes, requiring redoing the steps above. Always remember to backup important data before updating your system software. [mother is still in the process of dressing Nina] view a plan Students learn to express the meaning of Pi in words with this lesson 6, 7, 8 Title - What is Pi? By - Jill M. Snyder Primary Subject - Math Secondary Subjects - Grade Level - 6-8 Objective: At the end of this lesson the students will be able to describe in words what Pi is and means. Materials: different sized cylinders (one per student group), yarn, scissors, rulers. 1. Divide the students into pairs and assign them a group number. 2. Discuss with students what a diameter is and have each student group measure the diameter of their cylinder's base. Have them cut a piece of yarn the length of the diameter. 3. Have one student from each group place the measurement on the board under their group number. 4. Now have the students take their piece of yarn (Which is the diameter) and find a starting point somewhere on the cylinder. 5 Have the students wrap their piece of string around the cylinder and see how many times it will go around. 6. Now have the second partner go up to the board and write this number next to the measurement of their diameter. 7. Have the students look at the data. 8. They will notice that everyone had a different measurement for their diameter but everyone's yarn went around their cylinder the same amount of times. 9. Have the students brainstorm what this means and if it can happen for every circle. 10. Have students find random circles from around the classroom to see if it would work all the time. Expected Outcomes: The students will be able to discover what Pi (an abstract concept) means and actually is. I have done this with classes and they now will always remember what Pi means. Many of my students said "Pi is 3.14!" when I asked but they did not actually know what it meant. This lesson is great for a hands-on learning experience! E-Mail Jill M. Snyder ! We height get Mummy a belt for her car. Imagine you're playing a trivia game where you can earn points based on how many questions you answer correctly in a row. You get to calculate how many points you expect to earn on average! Let's see how we do it using our knowledge of probabilities. First, let's understand what "expected profit" means. It's like guessing how much candy you'll get after trick or treating in your neighborhood. Some houses may give you more candy than others, but when you consider the likelihood of visiting each house, you can estimate the total amount of candy you'll receive. Similarly, expected profit here refers to calculating the weighted sum of possible outcomes, considering their respective probabilities. Now, let's dive into the problem. Suppose you are answering n questions, one after another. Each question belongs to a unique category, and you know the probability p\_i of giving the correct answer to any given question i. The fun part begins now-for every stretch of k correct answers you give, you score k^4 points! Your goal is to compute the average number of points you would earn over time. Let's break down an example with three categories (n = 3), having probabilities p\_1 = 0.2, p\_2 = 0.3, and p\_3 = 0.4. To find the expected profit, follow these steps: 1. List out all the possible ways you could string together two or more correct answers. These include getting all three answers correct, combinations like "correct-correct-incorrect," "incorrect-correct-correct," etc., and finally just one single correct answer followed by incorrect ones. 2. Compute the product of probabilities associated with each combination listed in step 1. Remember, whenever you encounter a success (a correct answer), multiply its corresponding probability; otherwise, use (1 - probability) since there are infinitely many chances to fail compared to finite opportunities to succeed. 3. Multiply the products obtained in step 2 by the appropriate power of four, depending upon the length of the successful streak described by that particular scenario. This accounts for scoring rules wherein k correct answers yield k^4 points. 4. Sum up the values computed in step 3. Voila! That gives you the expected profit. In summary, figuring out the expected profit involves finding the combined value of various scenarios, taking into account the individual probabilities and point-scoring rules. So next time you play a trivia game or tackle similar problems, put on your math hat and start computing those exciting expected profits! " I will go into the zoo. Crump Lawndale Community Academy 3500 W. Douglas Chicago IL 60623 As a result of this lesson, students will be able to identify the colors of a rainbow using the mnemonic ROY G BIV. They will also recognize primary colors of light. The student will have an understanding of light blending to form new colors. Finally they will answer the question; Why is the sky blue? Retina Indigo Magenta Violet Primary Cyan 1. Three slide projectors or overheads, one of these should be a overhead. 2. Three color filters blue, red, yellow. 3. Various objects of bright colors. 4. Scissors, crayons, pencils each student will need these. 5. White cardboard. 6. Diffraction paper. 7. Construction paper, one sheet each of red, orange, yellow, green, blue indigo, and violet. 8. One glass of water, 1/2 teaspoon of coffee creamer. 9. A flashlight 10. A diagram of the human eye. The teacher will need to have a cardboard circle measuring 31/2" diameter for each student. Divide the circle into eight. The construction paper should be cut into arches to form a rainbow. Place the beginning letter of the color at the top of the arch i.e., R on the red, O on orange, etc. The three projectors should be set up in such a way that all three light beams will meet on a white screen. One color filter is placed near each projector. The diffraction paper should be cut into a small enough square to be tape over the light of the overhead. Spoon coffee creamer into water, do not stir let powder sink to bottom. Set aside for latter demonstration. Using the diagram of an eye explain the following: Light entering your eye hits the retina stimulating cones, then a message is sent to the brain telling you this is the color. The teacher will begin this activity by showing the students a rainbow. This is done with the previously describe overhead-diffration paper set up. Cut on projector, ask students to describe what colors they see. Next have students take the corresponding color and tape it to the board forming a rainbow. When this is complete ROY G BIV is viewed, the teacher should tell students this is the way to remember the colors of the rainbow. Place a red filter over one projector showing a red beam of light on a screen. Then do the same with the green filter. This should result in the two colors of light forming yellow on the screen. This should be done with red and blue to make magenta and blue plus green to make cyan. Explain that these colors are called primary colors of light because when combined they make up all the colors of the rainbow. The mnemonic for this is RGB. Finally combine all three to form white light. Next have students step in the path of white light, look at their shadow. What colors do you see? Place two filters on the overhead not over lapping. Then have students place an object in front of the light. Look at the color the object takes on. Discuss. Project a red light on the screen. Have students stare at it for 30 seconds. Turn off light. Do you still see a red screen? No the red cones in your retina are tired. The color you see is from the cones that are not tired. Why is the sky blue? The teacher will shine the flashlight onto a glass from above and move slowly down the back. Note the water color. Explain to the class that the particles of powder cream scatter the light from the flashlight. Putting the flashlight behind the glass causes the blue light to scatter aside allowing only orange-red light to be seen. Placing the light above the glass causes less light to scatter and more blue light is seen. Thus the water looks blue. When applied to the atmosphere, the flashlight is the sun, the water, the sky and the creamer are air particles. Give each student a wheel (circle), a red, blue, green crayon, pencil. Have the students color the wheel using two colors on each. Next push the pencil through the center. Spin the wheel to see a new colors. Give the students the following story. Their reply should use a rainbow diagram, name each color on it, tell why the sky is blue also give an explanation of primary colors used to form all colors. Once upon a time it was a rainy day. Once upon a time, two siblings, Jimmy and Amanda, were playing in the park. Jimmy was loading a rock into a truck, while Amanda was running around and making the prettiest of flowers with a piece of paper. Suddenly, Jimmy noticed a big pile of smelly trash near the fence and he called his sister Amanda. Amanda! Look at this, he shouted, pointing at the pile of trash, it's disgusting!" Amanda put her hand over her nose and said, Eeeew! What happened here? Let's clean this up! Jimmy and Amanda got to work and, one by one, they loaded the trash into a big bin, keeping their knee close to the ground. When the park was finally clean, both siblings felt great. They had worked together and taught each other the importance of taking care of the environment. The moral of the story is that when you work together, getting rid of anything disgusting can be easy. Title: How to Get a Spike in Animal Jam Animal Jam is a popular online game that allows players to create and customize their own animal characters and explore a virtual world filled with fun activities and social interactions. One way to make your character stand out is by obtaining rare items, such as the Spike clothing item. This article will provide you with an in-depth guide on how to get a Spike in Animal Jam. Step 1: Create an Account and Log In To start your journey towards getting a Spike in Animal Jam, you need to first create an account if you don't already have one. Visit the official Animal Jam website (www.animaljam.com) and click on the "Join Now" button. Follow the prompts to choose your animal, enter your birthday, and create a username and password. Once your account is set up, log in using your new credentials. Step 2: Earn Gems Gems are the primary currency in Animal Jam, which can be used to buy various items from the Diamond Shop or other players. To earn gems, complete daily challenges, participate in adventures, play mini-games, or sell unwanted items in your den. Keep in mind that earning gems takes time and patience, but having enough gems is essential to purchasing the Spike item. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Focus on completing daily challenges as they offer decent rewards and help you familiarize yourself with different aspects of the game. * Playing multiplayer games like Sarepia Penguins and Mt. Shiveer Ice Slides often results in higher gem earnings compared to single-player ones. * Be cautious when trading with other players; ensure that what you receive is worth more than what you give away. Step 3: Save Up Enough Gems The Spike item costs 500 gems at the Diamond Shop. Make sure you save up enough gems before proceeding further. It may take some time to accumulate this amount depending on how frequently you play and engage in money-making activities within the game. Step 4: Locate the Diamond Shop Once you have saved up enough gems, locate the Diamond Shop to purchase the Spike item. The Diamond Shop can be found inside Jamaa Township, near the center of the map. Look for a large building shaped like a diamond. Step 5: Enter the Diamond Shop Click on the entrance of the Diamond Shop once you find it. You will then be transported inside where you can browse through all sorts of unique and exclusive items available only for purchase with gems. Step 6: Buy the Spike Item Navigate through the Diamond Shop until you find the Clothing section. Inside, look specifically for the Hair category. Scroll down until you see the Spike option listed among other hairstyles. Click on it to select the item and add it to your shopping cart. Proceed to checkout and confirm your purchase using your collected gems. Congratulations! After following these steps carefully, you should now have successfully obtained a Spike in Animal Jam. Don't forget to equip it and show off your new style while exploring Jamaa! Did you know what these are called here? Once upon a time, there was a little girl who was busy learning new things. One day, she decided to go see Grandma and Grandpa. When she arrived, Grandma told her all about how she used to be busy when she was a kid. The command letters or words are called elements. Some elements have declared settings called properties. These properties help describe or shape the way the element will work or function. The property settings are known as values. Some tags can have many properties, others have none. |Note : HTML is not case sensitive. You can use upper and/or lower case letters. In some studies, it shows tags are easier to recognize when they are in upper case (capitalized). If you plan on going further into higher web page coding, it's best to have everything in lower case letters.| The first step in building a web page is entering the FOUNDATION tags. These are the ground level commands that make up the structure of the web page code. The general rule with HTML tags is : For every beginning, there is an ending. The tags shown with the forward slash in them / are the ending tags. - Hyper Text Markup Language. Most all web pages are written in this code. Did I just say code instead of language? Yes! The L does happen to symbolize language, but it referes to the English language. The page is created by the code and the code is written in English. HTML itself is not a language. - An introduction to the web page. In this area, some of the settings for the web page are introduced such as the TITLE, and information for search engines. - The title for the web page. Every good page deserves a name. This name will appear in the title bar at the top of the browser window. It is also used as the file name if someone 'bookmarks' your page or saves it. - Main program section for web page. Early Arrivals Activity 1. "You Are There" Coloring Sheet - Before class print out the Pattern and make copies. As your children work on the coloring sheet talk to them about how God knows everything about them such as, "He knows everything they are thinking and everything they are going to say before they even say it." 2. "You Are There" Itty Bitty Book - Before class print out the pages, cut them apart and staple them together according to the directions for Itty Bitty Books. In class have your children color the pictures and practice reading the book. Talk about the pictures and how God knows everything about them. For directions on how to print and assemble this book go to the Book Page. Last week we talked about how God knows everything about you. He knows when you sit and stand. He knows what you are thinking, and what you are going to say before you even say it. Like the Psalmist said, "God is so amazing that we will never really be able to understand how amazing he is". Have you ever gotten in trouble, or done something you weren't supposed to do? What did you think about when you knew your parents might find out? You probably wished you could hide from them, or hide the fact that you did something wrong so you wouldn't get punished. You can sometimes hide bad things from your parents, teachers, or friends, but you can never hide from God. Verse 7 says, (Open your Bible and read it.), "Whither shall I go from thy spirit? or whither shall I flee from thy presence?" meaning, "Where can I hide from you, and where can I run away from you? The psalmist is beginning to realize that it might not always be so great that God knows everything about him because God also sees the bad things we do. We can't hide from God and we can't run away. He continues, "If I ascend up into heaven, thou art there: if I make my bed in hell, behold, thou art there." The psalmist realizes that there is nowhere he can go to hide from God. Can you think of someone in the Bible who tried to flee from God? Jonah tried to flee from God when God told him to go to Nineveh and he didn't want to go. He ran away and got in a big ship that would take him in the opposite direction. But God knew exactly where Jonah was. He caused a big storm to come up. The ship was about to sink, and the crew was very scared. They started throwing everything over to lighten the ship. They all started praying to their gods for help, but the storm got even worse. Then they saw Jonah sleeping in a corner. They woke him up and asked him to pray with them. Jonah told them that the only thing that would save the ship was to throw him overboard because God was mad at him. When they threw him overboard the sea grew calm again. But God didn't forget about Jonah and let him drown; he sent a big fish to swallow him. And then he had the fish spit Jonah out on the shore. Jonah knew he had to obey God so he went to Nineveh as God had told him to do in the first place. Even in the mouth of a big fish at the bottom of the sea, God was with Jonah. Even when he disobeyed and did the wrong thing, God didn't leave him. The Psalmist experienced this same thing. He writes, (Read from your Bible), "If I take the wings of the morning, and dwell in the uttermost parts of the sea; Even there shall thy hand lead me, and thy right hand shall hold me. If I say, Surely the darkness shall cover me; even the night shall be light about me. Yea, the darkness hideth not from thee; but the night shineth as the day: the darkness and the light are both alike to thee." Father, thank you for being with us no matter where we are. We know that we cannot hide from you. You see everything we do, even the bad things. But even then, you guide us and lead us, and help us to do the right things. Forgive us for the wrong things we have done. Help us to be aware of your presence and to follow you. Amen. 1. Play "If I Go ___________ God is There." Have your children sit in a circle on the floor. Roll a small truck or car towards one of the children and say, "If I go to school, God is there." Tell the child who gets the truck to roll it to another child and say the same thing, except pick a different place. He may say something like, "If I go to the park, God is there." He then rolls the car to another child. Keep playing until all the children have had a turn. Have all the children sit in a circle. Start by saying, "If I go ___________, God is there." (Fill in the blank.) Then have the child on your right repeat what you said and then say the same thing only change the place. The next child would repeat the first two sentences and then add a new one. Keep playing until it comes back to you. 2. Action Rhyme God, where can I hide from you? - Look around to find somewhere to hide. Where can I run from your view? - Run around in a circle. If I go up to the heavens in the clear blue air, - Point up to the sky. You are there. If I make my bed deep down in the ground, - Get down on the ground. You know where I can be found. If I settle on the far side of the sea, - Pretend to swim walking around in a circle. You will be with me. You will hold my handand guide me. - Everyone holds hands and walks around in a circle. With me, you'll always be. God, you are with me everywhere, God, you are with me everywhere. You are with me. Have two adults or helpers stand facing each other holding hands with their arms held high forming a bridge. As you sing the song, have your children walk underneath the bridge and back around again. When you get to the last phrase, (You are with me.) the helpers should bring their hands down and capture the child that is walking under the bridge with their arms. The child who is captured should say one place where God is with him or her. Keep playing until all the children have had a turn. All rights reserved. To let children experience prejudice first hand, in reference to Martin Luther King Day. - A favorite class treat - enough for the entire class. I use peanut butter crackers or pretzels that we normally have for a morning snack. - Book: A Picture Book of Martin Luther King, Jr. or Happy Birthday Martin Luther King - Before I mention Martin Luther King, I pass out morning snacks to only half of my class. (This year I only gave them to the boys.) - When the other children question this, I explain that only the boys are getting snacks today. The rest of the class will be quite put out at this point. - I wait about 5-10 minutes and call them to our story rug, without any explanation for my actions. I start to read the picture book of Martin Luther King's life. - After I have completed reading the story, I ask the children who did not receive snacks how they felt about that. They are usually still very upset with me. - Then I ask those who did receive snacks how they felt. - Now I explain that I was demonstrating prejudice or unequal treatment. - The children have a greater understanding of how unfairly black people were treated in Mr. King's day. - Finally, I give morning snack to those children I skipped earlier. I have a worksheet made to send home so the parents know about our lesson. I explain what we did and have each child express their feelings during my lesson. As hard as it is to slight my children in this lesson, I feel that it is a real learning experience for them. Grade Level(s): K By: MB Farris, Kindergarten Teacher [laughing] 'cause your brothers play with them all the. "I      I can't find him, please, Jones," he stammered. "I      I looked everywhere!" Okay, at preschool, he talked about preschool and he said Look here I bet you could build a truck. She put the dime in the thing that you turn? The picture above shows the beginning of a song. After the 4 sharps that represent the key you see a symbol that is called the time signature or meter of the song. In this case it is the common which means that there are 4 quarter notes in a measure. A measure is the part of the staff between two vertical lines. Any of these means there are 4 beats in each measure. Count 1, 2, 3, 4. Both 3/4 and 6/8 have 3 beats in a measure. Count 1, 2, 3. This is a waltz. 2 beats in a measure. Count 1, 2. This is a march. Other meters are rare and you can define how long they are if you know that the upper value represents the number of notes that fit in a measure and the lower value the type of these notes. So 12/4 means 12 quarter notes in a measure. That is a waltz with a lot of notes in each measure. Each measure should be filled to it's full lenght with notes and rests. An exception is the first measure. As you can see in the picture at the top of this page, the first measure only contains a note 1 beat long. There are no rest symbols added before it. To play the notes at the right time you should count as the meter implies. In a 4/4 meter you must count in each measure: one - two - three - four like you are marching. You can play a quarter note at each count (beat). If there are 1/8 notes then count one -and - two - and - three - and - four -and in the same length of time. If there are 1/16 notes then count: one - po - ta - to - two - po - ta -to - three - po - ta - to - four - po - ta - to also in the same time as you would count 1-2-3-4. So you will have to say potato very fast. In the picture above you see how you must count two get the timing of the notes right. In the last measure you see how three notes are bundled in a triplet. That means three notes use the same time as two. I would add dal-da to the count to get the timing right. Usually the short flagged notes are beamed together into bundles that represent one count each. Each horizontal beam represents one flag like pictured below. A bow between notes means that these notes are played as one. So in this case you play E-F-G-A. The first E is played only once and lasts 5 beats. You count 1 (play E), 2, 3, 4, 1, 2 (play F), 3 (play G), 4 (play A). Back to the note reading homepage [sounds as if tape were turned off at this point] oh, you going to walk around with your spoon. BOOK THIRD V The two ladies who, in advance of the Swiss season, had been warned that their design was unconsidered, that the passes would not be clear, nor the air mild, nor the inns open--the two ladies who, characteristically, had braved a good deal of possibly interested remonstrance were finding themselves, as their adventure turned out, wonderfully sustained. It was the judgment of the head-waiters and other functionaries on the Italian lakes that approved itself now as interested; they themselves had been conscious of impatiences, of bolder dreams--at least the younger had; so that one of the things they made out together--making out as they did an endless variety--was that in those operatic palaces of the Villa d'Este, of Cadenabbia, of Pallanza and Stresa, lone women, however reinforced by a travelling-library of instructive volumes, were apt to be beguiled and undone. Their flights of fancy moreover had been modest; they had for instance risked nothing vital in hoping to make their way by the Br  nig. They were making it in fact happily enough as we meet them, and were only wishing that, for the wondrous beauty of the early high-climbing spring, it might have been longer and the places to pause and rest more numerous. Title: Understanding Parametric Curves through Roller Coasters and Slopes! Hey there, young mathematicians! Today we are going to learn about something called "parametric curves," but don't worry, it's not as scary as it sounds. In fact, you already have experience with this concept in your daily lives! Have you ever ridden a roller coaster? As the roller coaster moves along its tracks, its path can be described using parametric equations, just like those in our earlier example. The variable "t" represents time, so as time passes, the position of the roller coaster changes following specific rules - these rules are our parametric equations! Now let's talk about slopes. Imagine drawing a small hill on a piece of paper and placing a tiny ball on top of it. When you give the ball a gentle push, it will start rolling downhill. At each moment during its descent, the ball rolls along a certain line touching the hill - its instantaneous line of motion. This line has a slope, and that slope tells us whether the ball is moving quickly or slowly, turning left or right. We can calculate the slope of this line using some basic math concepts! Here's a fun exercise. Using a straight edge, draw several lines tangent to the graph of a parabola (it looks like a "U" shape). You should notice that all these lines touch the parabola at one single point, yet do not cross the parabola beyond that point. These tangent lines show us the local behavior of the parabola near that particular point. By finding the slope of these tangent lines, we gain insight into the speed and direction of change taking place at any given point on our graph! For parametric curves specifically, calculating the slope of the tangent line involves slightly more complex steps than simply finding the ratio between height and width like before. Instead, we first compute the derivatives dy/dt and dx/dt separately, then divide dy/dt by dx/dt. Don't fret over these new words; just remember that finding rates of change helps reveal important information about shapes and motions! What does having two tangents mean? Well, imagine tracing out two different paths leading towards the same destination. That's exactly what happens here. Even though both lines share the common meeting spot (in this case, the point (0,1)), they approach it via distinct routes, thus creating separate tangents. Finally, determining where the curve is concave upward or downward relates to understanding if the object represented by the curve is smiling or frowning. A happy face smiles while looking upwards, whereas a sad face turns downward. Similarly, when graphing mathematical functions, regions where the curve opens skyward indicate happiness, or concavity upward, while areas pointing groundward express sadness, or concavity downward. To make life easier, recalling our previous analogy involving the ball on the hill, whenever the ball switches from curving 'up' to curving 'down', that signifies concavity changing directions too! So there you have it! With these tools under your belt, you now possess the power to analyze fascinating graphs and uncover secrets hidden within them. Happy exploring! He says you do. For example, the halteres in flies help balance the insect while in flight, and the wings of ostriches are used in mating rituals, and in aggressive display. The ear ossicles in mammals are former bones of the lower jaw. = = = children/CHIDES_NA/HSLLD/HV2/TP/bobtp2.cha = = = Open the can and pour it in the pot. However, there are still many users who continue to utilize this OS. One common issue faced by these users is the inability to boot their system due to a missing HAL.DLL file. This problem can be frustrating as it may seem like all data is lost; however, with careful steps, you can fix this issue without losing any data. Note: at the end of the session, you can ask how many of these feelings they think Fido has. Talk about examples of how dogs might feel like people. (can be adapted for Grades 4-12) This activity can help open up communication with and among students about their feelings. It also helps build students' vocabulary and their confidence in using their full vocabulary range. When students are challenged to come up with words in a game of charades, they may demonstrate that they know more words than they thought they did. The point of "Feelings Charades" isn't just guessing the right words - much of the lesson is in the "wrong" guesses students make. You will need enough 3x5 index cards for every student in the class. On each card, write a feeling. Try to balance "positive" feelings (excited, proud, elated) with "negative" ones (frustrated, furious, nervous). Try to steer away from the simplest, most common terms (sad, happy, mad). 1. Class Discussion about Expressing Feelings You might begin the activity by talking with students about the importance of expressing our feelings in a constructive way - and of understanding others' feelings. Expressing and acknowledging feelings is an important tool for resolving conflicts. And trying to understand another person's feelings can help deescalate conflict. When someone doesn't use words to say what they're feeling, we often try to guess what they feel by interpreting their facial expression or their body language. Today, we're going to see how well we are able to interpret someone's feelings this way. In Feelings Charades, students will have a chance to demonstrate one emotion. The rest of the class will guess what the emotion is. The person who is doing the charade cannot speak - they must use facial expressions or body language to get classmates to guess the emotion. After the game, we'll have a class-wide discussion about what happened. 2. Select Scribes Select two volunteers to be "scribes." Note: If your class has more than 20-25 students, you may want to create two groups for the charades game, each with two scribes. The scribes will take turns writing down every guess students make in the course of each charade. They will alternate turns in taking notes. Whoever is not taking notes during a charade can participate in the game. Tell the scribes that the class will be using their notes when the charades are over. 3. Demonstrate a Charade Demonstrate a charade, choosing whatever emotion you like. Begin the charade by passing both hands in front of your face, bringing them down as if a curtain is dropping over your face. Once your hands have passed your chin, show the emotion. (This gesture can help charaders clear the slate and focus on their performance.) Let students guess what emotion you are depicting. If they don't guess the emotion in a minute or two, tell them. 4. Class Charades If possible, give every student a chance to perform a brief charade -- or, if not, ask for a certain number of volunteers. Ask each performer to select a card from the collection in your hand. They should begin with the curtain, then use only facial expressions and body language to demonstrate their word. Check to make sure the scribes understand their job and are carrying it out. Move quickly until everyone has performed a charade. 5. Post-Game Discussion Afterwards, have discussion with students about what happened. You might ask: Did you like the game? What was fun about it? What didn't you like? What did you notice about the game? Did we always guess the right word? Did we often guess the same words over and over? Which ones? This last question is an opportunity to discuss the "ad" family of feelings: mad, sad, bad, glad. These words (along with "happy" and "good") tell us something, but not as much as more precise words would. 6. Reviewing the Class's Work Then, ask the scribes to read off some of the words they have written down. You might write these on the board. When a good number have been listed, you might remark on the wide range of words students have come up with. We know more words than we think we do, although sometimes we don't use all the words we know. Make sure you collect the scribes' complete lists - you may be able to use these word collections in follow-up activities, like those described below. 7. Follow-up Activities Post a list of all the words the scribes wrote down prominently in the classroom as a reminder of all the "feelings" words students have at their disposal. At any point, you might pick a word and ask students to come up with synonyms and antonyms. You might also select words in the "ad" family (or "nice" or "good") and ask students to create a "thermometer" showing the range of feelings these words cover. For instance, a "mad" thermometer might go from "irritated" on the cool end to "enraged" on the hot end. "Sad" might go from "disappointed" to "grief-stricken." The feelings words might also be used as a starting point for writing assignments. In 2008, archaeologists discovered a bone flute in the Hohle Fels cave near Ulm, Germany. The five-holed flute has a V-shaped mouthpiece and is made from a vulture wing bone. The researchers involved in the discovery officially published their findings in the journal "Nature", in June 2009. The discovery is also the oldest confirmed find of any musical instrument in history. Other flutes were also found in the cave. This flute was found next to the Venus of Hohle Fels and a short distance from the oldest known human carving. When they announced their discovery, the scientists suggested that the "finds demonstrate the presence of a well-established musical tradition at the time when modern humans colonized Europe". An activity from Dave Mastie (Ann Arbor, MI) Type of Lesson: Demonstration/Discussion Time Needed: 20 minutes Physical Science, Grades K-4: Light travels in a straight line until it strikes an object. Light can be reflected by a mirror, refracted by a lens, or absorbed by the object. Physical Science, Grades 5-8: Light interacts with matter by transmission (including refraction), absorption, or scattering (including reflection). To see an object, light from the object-emitted by or scattered from it-must be enter the eye. Physical Science, Grades 5-8: The sun is a major source of energy for changes on the earth's surface The sun's energy arrives as light with a range of wavelengths, consisting of visible light, infrared, and ultraviolet radiation. 2. Tape the pieces of paper in place with masking tape so they won't move. 3. Now tape the holographic diffraction grating on the upper, outward facing lens of the projector (curved lens on arm of projector, but not the lens that points down). 4. Now turn on the projector and you should see a spectrum of colors displayed on the wall or screen or wherever the projector is pointing. B. Separating/Displaying the Spectrum (see image that will help in the building of the stack of blocks) 1. Normal paraffin wax should come with 4 blocks in it. Take one of the full blocks of paraffin wax and create an imprint with it on a single sheet of aluminum foil. Use the scissors to cut out this imprinted portion of aluminum foil. Now cut 8 more pieces this size. Place to the side. 2. Now, using the knife cut the 4 wax blocks at the halfway point creating 8 blocks that are approximately 2 1/4 inch x 4 1/2 inch x 1/2 inch thick. You may want to just score the blocks at the halfway point, then just break the blocks using the edge of a desk or table. 3. Fold your aluminum foil pieces in half (dull side in). They should now be the same size of the wax blocks you have cut in half. 4. Place wax blocks and aluminum foil in a stack. Start with an aluminum piece on bottom, then a block, then aluminum foil, then a block, then aluminum foil...until the last wax block is added to the top of stack, topping with an aluminum foil piece. The wax blocks should be stacked so that the "front" of the stack has all smooth faces. It doesn't matter if the back of the stack has some blocks with rough surfaces (from the cutting or breaking of the blocks). 5. Now use the masking tape to secure the block stack together. For extra security, place rubber bands on top of tape. (You want to leave the smooth side facing front without tape or rubber bands on it). 6. Now hold the block stack in front of your projector. You'll want to hold the block stack about 2 feet from the projector. The spectrum of colors should "get caught" in the block stack with each block lighting up a different color! This sure will be a surprise for students and yourself! Paraffin wax can be found at craft or kitchen stores. The sales people may not know it is called paraffin wax, but they are sure to recognize gulf wax or canning wax (it's all the same stuff!). The holographic grating is a good alternative to prisms which can be finicky in experiments and demonstrations. Use this demonstration with younger students having them draw and label the block stack with the colors in it. Use this demonstration to start a discussion with older students with regards to the spectrum, wavelengths, diffraction and how the colors are being trapped in each wax block by the reflecting aluminum foil. This is a good lifelong teaching tool - once you build the wax block stack, it can be used for many years to come! " "Why, William," said his mother, "you're not going to die, so why talk about it?" That's so you don't lose any of it. Title: Fall Asleep Faster Using an Old MP3 Player Introduction: Having trouble falling asleep? You're not alone. Many people struggle with insomnia or simply unwinding after a long day. One simple and effective solution is using an old MP3 player as a sleep aid. This tutorial will guide you through the process, providing detailed explanations and helpful tips along the way. What you need: - An old MP3 player (preferably one without a screen) - A pair of headphones or earbuds - Preloaded relaxing music, white noise, or guided meditation tracks Step 1: Charge your MP3 player Make sure your device has enough battery life for at least eight hours of continuous playback. This ensures that you have ample time to fall asleep and allows the sound to continue playing throughout the night if needed. Step 2: Select calming content Populate your MP3 player with soothing sounds such as classical music, ambient noise, nature sounds, or guided meditations. These types of audio can help create a peaceful environment conducive to sleep. Avoid stimulating content like upbeat music, podcasts, or audiobooks, which may keep you awake. Key tip: Some popular options include brown noise, rain sounds, binaural beats, and isochronic tones. Experiment with different genres to find what works best for you. Step 3: Set up your listening equipment Choose comfortable headphones or earbuds that won't cause discomfort during extended use. Opt for ones designed for sleeping, ideally with soft padding and adjustable volume control. Wireless models are preferred to prevent tangling while changing positions in bed. Guideline: If possible, avoid connecting the MP3 player directly to external speakers since they might disturb others in the room. Step 4: Adjust settings on your MP3 player Turn off any features that emit light, including backlights and equalizer displays. Lower the brightness level if your MP3 player doesn't have an option to turn it off completely. Enable shuffle mode to mix up the order of songs, ensuring consistent background noise and preventing sudden silence from waking you up. Step 5: Create a bedtime routine Incorporate your new sleep aid into a regular pre-sleep ritual. Dim the lights, perform relaxation exercises, read a book, or engage in other calming activities before going to bed. This routine signals your body that it's time to wind down and prepare for sleep. Key tip: Keep electronic devices out of reach during this period to minimize distractions and maintain focus on relaxation techniques. Step 6: Test your setup Try out your new sleep aid for short periods during naps or early evenings when you don't plan on sleeping for a full night. Observe its effects on your ability to relax and drift off. Make necessary adjustments based on these observations. Step 7: Use consistently Establish a habit of using your MP3 player every night. Consistency reinforces positive associations between the calming sounds and sleep, making it easier to nod off quickly over time. Conclusion: Using an old MP3 player as a sleep aid is a cost-effective, customizable solution for those seeking better quality rest. By carefully selecting appropriate content, setting up comfortable listening equipment, and integrating the device into a bedtime routine, you too can enjoy faster, deeper slumber. Sweet dreams!
