John would organize a game of tag and the children would laugh and giggle as they ran around. John would also organize a hide and seek game. Everyone would hide behind the carpet. Then, when the seeker found them, they would all burst into laughter. At the end of the day, John would roll up the carpet and take it home, looking forward to the next day when he could bring back his favorite carpet again. Title: How to Deal with Attention Seekers ========================================= Attention seekers can be draining and challenging to handle, especially if you're not sure how to respond to their behavior. However, by understanding why people seek attention and implementing effective strategies, you can maintain healthy relationships while setting boundaries. This wikiHow will guide you through dealing with attention seekers in various contexts. ### Understanding Attention Seeking Behavior Before diving into the steps, let's examine what attention-seeking behavior is and why people engage in it. **Definition**: Attention seeking refers to behaviors aimed at gaining other people's focus, approval, or validation. These actions may include exaggerating stories, constantly talking about themselves, acting out, or creating drama. People who frequently exhibit these behaviors might have low self-esteem, lack emotional support, crave connection, or struggle with mental health issues like anxiety or depression. Key Tips and Guidelines ----------------------- * **Empathy**: Try to understand where the person is coming from before reacting. Their need for attention could stem from unmet needs or deeper personal struggles. * **Patience**: Changing attention-seeking patterns takes time. Stay consistent in your approach without losing your temper. * **Self-care**: Protect your own wellbeing by establishing firm boundaries and taking breaks when needed. ### Step 1: Identify Attention-Seeking Behaviors = = = children/CHIDES_NA/HSLLD/HV3/ER/rauer3.cha = = = The um what's his name ordered a cheeseburger a vanilla milkshake and a coke. Fin whale, common rorqual Fin whales are the second largest animal in the world - only blue whales are larger. May be two - Northern and Southern. Fin whales can be up to 26m long (Northern Hemisphere population is smaller), and weigh 30-80 tonnes. Fin whales are brown/grey above and white on their undersides. Their lower lip is white and the rest of head is black or grey. They have a greyish-white chevron mark on their backs, and a single longitudinal ridge on the head. The fin slopes back. Fin whales have a worldwide distribution, but they are not common in tropical seas and polar seas with ice. Fin whales feed on a variety of planktonic animals as well as crustaceans, squid and fish. Fin whales tend to live in groups of 3-7 individuals, but can sometimes be found migrating in groups of 300. Fin whales are classified as Endangered by the 2000 IUCN Red List. Global threats include hunting, prey depletion, pollution and collisions with vessels. I think he said you can write a letter to anyone that you want. It isn't XXX it looks like water. [How could Mommy be gone when she's supposed to be upstairs working on her terminal] Monday, February 4, 2013 First Grade - Sentence Structure Action Research In Mrs. Percival's first grade, we have been working on an action research project. The goal for this art lesson was to help the children with their writing skills with an art correlation. To do this, Mrs. Percival reviewed the basic parts of a sentence before they came to a special art class. When the class came to art, we did a brief review and each child selected a postcard of an animal they wanted to draw. We used Crayola Color sticks and I gave them a basic lesson in observational drawing. This is a difficult skill, but the children did a great job! First they drew the basic shape of the animal, added the details and colored in a background. We started running out of time, so towards the end of class, Mrs. Percival and I helped each child as they wrote a sentence about their animal. The goal was for each child to write a complete sentence using a noun, a verb, at least one adjective and proper punctuation. Some of the sentences are more complex than others. The little bobcat is climbing a log. The fuzzy wolf is howling. The little raccoon is studying the meadow. The huge eagle is soaring in the sky. The little baby fox is licking. The huge brown elk is bugling. This was a fun project and we hope it gave them a BOOST in their understanding of sentence structure. The children will be taking a standardized test soon, and we are interested to see if this activity might have helped. We will let you know what the results are! [MOT places her silver man and then Chi puts his on top of the pyramid] Task-based grammar teaching Lindsay Clandfield provides tips and ideas for task-based grammar teaching. One approach to teaching language that has attracted a lot of attention over the past twenty-five years is a task based approach to learning and teaching. In task-based approaches, the focus of classroom activities is on the task, and ultimately on meaning (for more on Task Based Teaching and Learning, see the Methodology section). In Jane Willis' flexible model for task-based learning, learners begin by carrying out a communicative task, without specific focus on form. After they have done the task, they report and discuss how they accomplished this, perhaps listening to a fluent speaker doing the same task. Only at the end is there a specific focus on features of language form. The advantage of the task-based approach, according to its advocates, is that during the task the learners are allowed to use whatever language they want, freeing them to focus entirely on the meaning of their message. This makes it closer to a real-life communicative situation. One danger of task-based approaches is that focus on meaning could come at the expense of focus on form. Learners become quite fluent, but their utterances are not often accurate. In addition, they develop strategies to complete the tasks quite quickly, cutting corners in their language use and form. Nevertheless, the task-based model is an attractive and liberating one, especially if you and your learners have been accustomed to a Presentation - Practice - Production model. The attached lesson plan follows Jane Willis' flexible task-based learning framework* to teach the grammar point used to. Students discuss how they were different 10 years ago. - For this class you need a photograph of yourself when you were 10 years younger (or thereabouts). This works best if you look considerably different in the photo than you do now. - You also need to prepare a short text about how your life was different then to what it is now. In this text include at least two examples of things you used to do and two examples of things you didn't use to do. You can either record this text onto a cassette to play for the students, or practise reading it out loud until you are able to "speak" it comfortably in front of the class. - Prepare a written version of this text that you can distribute to the class. Aim: To prepare students for the task, to engage their attention. Tell the students that you are going to show them a photo of you from 10 years ago. Ask them what they think will be different, but don't correct them at this stage (i.e. respond to the meaning of what they say, not the form). Allow time for three or four suggestions. Then take out the photo of yourself and walk around the class, showing it to the students. Ask them what was different about you then. Put the picture up on the board and ask "What else was different about my life, do you think?" Allow more comments and suggestions from the class, but don't tell them if they are right or wrong in their guesses. Explain that they will find this out later. Aim: For students to discuss how their life was different ten years ago. Ask students to work in groups of three. Tell them to talk about their life ten years ago. Put the following questions on the board: - What did you look like? - What was different about your life? - Did you have different likes and dislikes? Different hobbies? - Are you very different now? Explain that the questions are to help them start talking. Give them a time limit of three to five minutes to discuss this. Circulate and listen to the students doing the task, but do not correct any language at this moment. Aim: For students to prepare an oral report of their task. Stop the task. Tell the students that they must work together to prepare a summary of their discussion to report to the whole class. They must write notes for this summary and be prepared to report this orally to the rest of the class. Set a time limit of five minutes for them to do this. Aim: For students to present their reports and find out who was most different ten years ago. When the students are ready, ask a spokesperson from each group to report the group's summary. Tell the class that they must listen to each group's report and decide at the end of all the reports which students have changed the most in the past ten years.After all the reports, ask students who they think has changed the most. You could ask the students who have changed the most to bring in a photo of themselves at that time. Aim: For students to hear a fluent English speaker doing the same task. Draw the student's attention back to the photo of you on the board and explain that you are going to ask them to listen to you doing the same task that they did.Read or play the recording that you made. Ask the students some quick comprehension questions about what they heard (e.g. What did I say about my hair? What did I say about my job?) If the students find it difficult to understand, repeat the text again. Aim: To raise students' awareness about the target language. Choose two or three sentences from your text which include the grammar "used to" and write them on the board. For example: a) I used to go to heavy metal concerts. b) I used to have long black hair. c) I used to wear tight leather trousers. d) I didn't use to do my homework. Check that students understand the meaning of the sentences. This is best done by concept check questions. For example a) above, the concept check questions would be: - Did I go to heavy metal concerts in the past? (Yes) - Was it a regular occurrence? (Yes) - Do I go to heavy metal concerts now? (No). Explain the rules of form for used to: - used to + infinitiveand - didn't use to + infinitive At this point you could distribute the script of your story and ask students to find other examples of how you used used to. Point out that in English we use used to to talk about states and habits that continued for a period of time in the past. Aim: To give the students some restricted written practice in the target language. If you feel that your class needs some restricted practice in the grammar, ask them to write down three things that they used to do and three things that they didn't use to do when they were children. Circulate and monitor. Ask students to check their sentences with each other and elicit some examples to put on the board. Aim: To give the students a chance to repeat (and hopefully improve) the task. Ask students to work with a partner that they haven't worked with yet during this class. Tell them to repeat the same task as they did at the beginning of the class, but that they should try to include the target structure used to into their speaking. Once students have practised together, ask a couple to report back to the class what they talked about with their partner. Use this time to focus on accuracy, i.e. correct what they say if they make mistakes using the target language. = = = children/CHIDES_NA/Gilliam/TD/6m/46071v.cha = = = In November 2018, Presley received posthumous the Presidential Medal of Freedom from President Donald Trump. The first and broadest sense of "art" means "arrangement" or "to arrange." In this sense, art is created when someone arranges things found in the world into a new or different design or form; or when someone arranges colors next to each other in a painting to make an image or just to make a pretty or interesting design. [at one point Nola had lost sight of Nina for five minutes and Nina had gotten very upset.] A bull is an intact (i.e., not castrated) adult male of the species "Bos taurus". [24 sec silence, S mumbles to himself 3 times, sound play at 3, silence] Title: How to Delete Application Data in iOS Deleting application data in iOS can help free up storage space on your device and improve its overall performance. This tutorial will guide you through the process of deleting app data on your iPhone or iPad, providing detailed explanations for each step along with key tips and guidelines. ### Step 1: Access Settings Tap on the grey gear icon labeled "Settings" from your home screen or app library. The settings app allows you to customize various aspects of your device's functionality and manage stored data. ### Step 2: Navigate to General Settings In the settings menu, scroll down until you find the "General" option (it should be the fourth item from the top) and tap on it. Here, you'll find several general settings related to your device's operation, including storage management. ### Step 3: Go to Storage & iCloud Usage Under the 'General' section, look for 'Storage & iCloud Usage'. Tapping this option will take you to a new page where you can view details about your used and available storage as well as manage individual apps' data. ### Step 4: Manage App Storage On the 'Storage & iCloud Usage' page, select 'Manage Storage' under the 'STORAGE' header. You'll now see a list of all installed applications sorted by their size consumption. ### Step 5: Choose an Application Grade School Guide to the Gaussian Distribution ============================================== Have you ever wondered why sometimes things seem to follow a pattern or happen more often than not? Or perhaps you've noticed that certain events tend to cluster around an average value? Well, there's actually a mathematical concept that describes this phenomenon called the "Gaussian Distribution." Don't worry if that sounds complicated - we're going to break it down into easy-to-understand concepts! Imagine you have a bag full of jelly beans. Some are red, some are blue, but most of them are green. Now, suppose you reach your hand into the bag and pull out one jelly bean after another, keeping track of their colors. You notice something interesting: even though you get all sorts of different colored jelly beans, green ones are by far the most common. Over time, you may start seeing a trend where green jelly beans show up way more frequently than any other color. This idea of having one outcome occur more often than others is exactly what statisticians call a "probability distribution." Now let's take our jelly bean example further. Instead of just counting the number of each color, imagine measuring the weight of each individual jelly bean using a tiny scale. After weighing dozens, maybe even hundreds of jelly beans, you realize something fascinating: while there's still plenty of variation when it comes to size, many of these weights are centered around a specific point-let's say two grams per jelly bean. So now, instead of looking at frequencies like before, we're examining values clustered around an average weight. And guess what? That too follows its own special kind of distribution, which happens to resemble the shape of a bell. In fact, mathematicians refer to it as the "Bell Curve!" Here's where things really get interesting. The Bell Curve isn't exclusive to jelly beans; it shows up everywhere in nature! Take human heights, shoe sizes, test scores-you name it. When plotted on a graph with enough data points, these variables create a familiar curved line, peaking near the middle and tapering off towards both ends. This particular curve represents what statisticians call the "Gaussian Distribution," named after German mathematician Johann Carl Friedrich Gauss who studied it extensively during his lifetime. So next time you find yourself wondering whether certain outcomes are merely coincidental or part of a larger pattern, remember the Gaussian Distribution. It reminds us that even within seemingly chaotic systems, order can emerge given enough observations. Whether it's jelly beans, heights, or anything else under the sun, keep an eye out for those bells curves! They'll always lead you back to the beauty of mathematics hidden behind the scenes. In the 3D coordinate system there is a third axis, and in equations there is a third variable. We will work with vectors in the 3D coordinate system and learn how to interpret the coordinates an of a vector in the 3D coordinate system. With the introduction to the 3D coordinate system, we also encounter other vector operations, lines and planes. I want to talk about the 3 dimensional coordinate system. Here I have a picture of a 3 dimension coordinate system it's got 3 axes, the x axis, the y axis and the z axis. And this is the typical orientation with the x axis sort of coming out at you, the y axis going to the right and the z axis going up. So the x, y plane that you're used to is lying flat, now how do you plot points in a 3 d coordinate system? Let's start by plotting point a which has coordinates 1, 2 and 5 since there are 3 axes we need 3 coordinates and it's going to be x, y, z, 1, 2 and 5. So first I look for x=1 and that's here and then y=2 and that's here and I find the point in the x, y plane that they correspond to and that's right here. And then for the third coordinate 5 I just go up 5 units, so this is unit I go 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and this is going to be my point a. Let's try another 1, b which is 4, 6, 2 first thing I do is I locate 4 on the x axis that's 1, 2, 3 4 I locate 6 on the y axis 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 is right here so four 6 is down here right on the x, y plane. Now from here I need to go up 2 units this is how long a unit is so I go vertically up 1 and then 2 and that's going to be my point b which is 4, 6, 2. So that's basically how points are plotted in a 3 d coordinate system. Now let's talk about vectors in the coordinate system, specifically positioned vectors now remember in 2 dimensions a position vector is a vector that goes from the origin to a point p and it's exactly the same thing in 3 dimensions. So let's draw the position vector o, p goes from the origin to point p which has coordinates x, y and z. Now I want to find, I want to be able to write the components of a vector like this and the components of a position vector will always be just x, y and z the coordinates of the point where vector terminates. Now I want to find the length of this vector op and I can do it using what I know about 2 dimensional vectors. Let's talk about the point that is right below it in the x, y plane point a. Point a is going to have coordinates x, y and 0 right all the points in the x, y plane have a z coordinate of 0. So this will be x, this will be y and this tells me that, this distance here can be found using the Pythagorean Theorem what that distance is, is the length of vector oa. So oa its length squared is going to be x squared plus y squared, x is this length and y is this length so x squared plus y squared. Now secondly let me take a look at vector ap, ap goes straight up from point a in the xy plane to point p and its length is z. So the length of op squared, the length of this length squared is going to be this length squared plus this length squared because this is a right triangle and that's hard to see when you have it drawn in a perspective like this. But this is a right angle, there's any vector pointing straight up is going to be perpendicular to any vector lying flat in the xy plane. Alright so is going to be equal to oa squared plus ap squared. Now we already found oa squared it's x squared plus y squared and ap squared is just z squared. And here we have it, the magnitude of op squared is x squared, plus y squared, plus z squared. So the magnitude of op is the square root of x squared plus y squared, plus z squared. This magnitude not only gives you the length of the position vector op it also gives you the distance from this point to the origin. So the distance from point p to the origin is x squared, the square root of x squared plus y squared, plus z squared. Teaching Fraction Addition A response to the question: Might you have any ideas as to what I can present fraction addition using some form of technology or physical manipulation? My goal is to demonstrate my ingenuity in the use of "hands on" objects to teach the concepts of adding numerical fractions.------------- Have you ever had students make fractions strips, and then use them to compare fractional amounts? In case you haven't here is a quick set of directions. (You could also buy these materials, but if a child makes them, that is part of the learning.) Cut a set of strips that are all the same size. You can do that by using lined paper and cutting them so that the lines are running vertically through the strips ( up and down). If you make each strip 24 lines long, that will make some of the folding easier, too. Now, label the first strip "one whole". It will be worth one. Take another strip, fold it into two equal pieces, and label each one "one half" (you can also use the fraction 1/2, to help your students link the word and the symbol). Take another strip, and fold it into four equal pieces (to be labeled fourths). There is more than one way to do these folds. To make it easier to compare them later, making the folds vertically, so the segments are all in line, is best. It is a good idea to let your students see how many different ways they can make these folds to create "equal" pieces, though. Continue to make all the fraction pieces from halves to twelfths. Some will be more difficult to make than others. Working through the activity will help your students see the relationships between the fractions though. Resist the temptation to do it for them so the materials look neat... When you are done, you can use these strips to compare fractions to see how many of one strip make the same sized piece as another strip. You can also compare to see which pieces are larger, or smaller than other pieces, for example, 12 is smaller than 5/8 and 8/12. Finally, you can use them to add and subtract fractions. Suppose you want to add three fourths and one half. You find the one half strip, and the fourths strip. Lay them side by side to notice that one half is the same as two fourths. Now lay them one after the other, like a long train. You have the half, which is the same as two fourths, and the three fourths. That is five fourths all together. It is longer that the one whole strip, so it must be worth more than one. you can compare to find out how much longer than one it is. If the fraction were 4 sixths and 3 fourths, you could still add them. Put them like a train, and note the length. Find a strip that compares with both the sixths and the fourths ( twelfths work) Change the names of the two fractions into twelfth, by comparing. Then figure out by substituting how many twelfths you have... -Gail, for the T2T service Join a discussion of this topic in T2T. Choosing an Internet Relay Chat (IRC) client can be a daunting task given the variety of options available. This tutorial will guide you through selecting an appropriate IRC client that suits your needs by breaking down the process into manageable steps with clear explanations and helpful tips. **Step 1: Identify Your Needs and Preferences** Before choosing an IRC client, consider what features are important to you. Some common factors include cross-platform compatibility, user interface preferences, support for multiple servers or channels, scripting capabilities, and security features. By identifying these requirements early on, you'll ensure that the chosen client aligns with your expectations. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Consider whether you need a lightweight option for basic chatting or more advanced functionality such as file transfers, customizable scripts, or theming options. * Think about where you plan to use this software - at home, work, both? If so, look for clients that offer multi-platform support (Windows, MacOS, Linux). **Step 2: Research Popular IRC Clients** There are numerous IRC clients across different platforms; some popular ones include mIRC, HexChat, Quassel, WeeChat, and XChat. A quick online search will yield reviews and comparisons between various clients which can help narrow down your choices based on usability, reliability, and community feedback. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Read user testimonials and expert opinions to understand strengths and weaknesses of each client. * Check if the client is still actively maintained and updated regularly, indicating ongoing development and support. **Step 3: Evaluate Each Option Based On Criteria From Step One** Once you have shortlisted potential clients, evaluate them against the criteria identified in Step One. For example, if having a modern UI is crucial, eliminate any clients from your list that lack this feature. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Prioritize must-have features over nice-to-haves when making your decision. * Keep in mind future growth; even though certain features may not currently be necessary, they might become relevant later. **Step 4: Download and Install The Selected Client** After deciding on a specific IRC client, download it from its official website or trusted source. Follow the installation prompts carefully to avoid any issues during setup. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Always choose standard or recommended settings unless there's a compelling reason to do otherwise. * Remember to uncheck any preselected third-party software bundled with the installer if you don't want it installed. **Step 5: Configure The Client To Match Your Requirements** Customize the settings of the IRC client according to your preferences. Common configurations include server connections, nicknames, preferred theme, etc. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Document changes made to the default configuration for easy troubleshooting and reconfiguration later. * Test out different themes and layouts to find one most comfortable for extended usage. **Step 6: Familiarize Yourself With Basic Commands And Functions** Spend time learning fundamental commands like joining rooms, sending private messages, changing topics, and managing users. Most clients provide documentation or tutorials within their websites or via help menus inside the application itself. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Bookmark useful resources related to command references and FAQ sections of the chosen client. * Practice using the client gradually until confident and proficient with essential functions. By following these six steps, you should now have an IRC client tailored to meet your communication needs effectively! Bonnie wanted grandma to bake cookies, and she wanted to help her. Please explain how to figure out the following 7th grade math problem: If a rubber ball bounces exactly 1/2 the height from which it is dropped; and it is dropped from the top of a building that is 64 meters tall, how high will the ball bounce on its eighth bounce? 64(1/2 + 1/4 + 1/8 + 1/16 + 1/32 + 1/64 + etc. Get the idea? In algebra terms, you could look at the problem like this: A. Set your variables. Do this by thinking about which parts of the problem can change. For this problem, there is the height of the final bounce, the height of the building, and the number of bounces. 1) h = Height of final bounce 2) b = Height of building 3) n = Number of bounces B. Look for the pattern (see tchrwill's example above). Looks like in the fraction, the numerator "1" stays the same. The denominator changes though, as follows. 1 bounce = 1/2 2 bounces = 1/4 3 bounces = 1/8 4 bounces = 1/16 If you look closely, denominator seems to be powers of 2, such as 2exp(1) = 2; 2exp(2) = 4; 2exp(3) = 8; 2exp(4) = 16; and so on. C. Try making a formula. Based on the numbers above, the formula should probably be: h = b x [1/2exp(n)] Just think through this formula step by step and you'll see how I got that. D. Test it (see if the formula works). For the eighth bounce, we have h = b x [1/2exp(n)] h = 64m x [1/2exp(8)] Note that 2exp(8) = 2 x 2 x 2 x 2 x 2 x 2 x 2 x 2. Multiply those out and you get 256. h = 64m x [1/256] Note that 1/256 approximately equals 0.004. Get this by long division or by calculator. h = 64m x 0.004 h = 0.256m So on the eighth bounce, the ball should bounce about 256mm. You should also plug in n = 1 and n = 2 just to satisfy yourself that the final answer is right. Hope that helps! Physics - A rubber ball dropped from a height of exactly 5 ft bounces (hits the ... Title: How to Delete a Video on Vine Vine is a popular social media platform that allows users to share short video clips. However, there may be situations where you want to delete a video you've uploaded. This tutorial will guide you through the process of deleting a vine in simple, easy-to-follow steps. Step 1: Open the Vine App Start by opening the Vine app on your mobile device. If you are not already logged in, enter your login credentials to access your account. Key Tip: Make sure you have a stable internet connection to ensure smooth functioning of the app. Step 2: Navigate to Your Profile Tap on the person icon located at the bottom right corner of the screen. This will take you to your profile page where all your vines are displayed. Guideline: The profile page gives an overview of your activity on Vine including number of loops (views), followers, and following count. Step 3: Locate the Video You Want to Delete Scroll down through your vines until you find the one you wish to remove. Once found, tap on it to open the individual video page. Key Point: Each vine has its own unique URL and can be shared independently via other platforms like Twitter and Facebook. Step 4: Access the More Options Menu On the opened video page, look for three dots or more symbol usually placed at top right corner. Tapping this will reveal a dropdown menu with various options. Step 5: Select 'Delete Post' Option From the list of available options, choose 'Delete Post'. A confirmation dialog box will appear asking if you really want to delete the vine. Step 6: Confirm Deletion To finalize the removal process, press 'OK' or 'Yes, Delete' when prompted. After confirming, the selected vine should no longer be visible on your profile nor accessible elsewhere on the site. Precautions: Deleted vines cannot be recovered. So make absolutely certain before proceeding with deletion. Also note that deleting a vine does not affect reposts made by others. In Conclusion, deleting a vine involves navigating to the specific vine within your profile, accessing the more options menu, selecting 'delete post', and finally confirming the action. Remember, deleted vines are gone forever so only delete what you're sure about. His mothers told him it was time to go to school. oh, yes he has them too Ross. oh, I thought you were all through. Infinity is usually not an actual number, but it is sometimes used as one. Infinity often says how "many" there is of something, instead of how "big" something is. For example, there are infinitely many whole numbers (called integers), but there is no integer which is infinitely big. But different kinds of math have different kinds of infinity. So its meaning often changes. And the mom said at least did you get to play on the equipment? No I don't wanna talk about it. oh, is that the color of your snowsuit jacket? Today, we're going to learn about something called "scientific notation," which is a fancy way of writing really big (or really small!) numbers more easily. You know how when you multiply a number by 10, all you have to do is move the decimal point one place to the right? Well, scientific notation uses that idea to make it easier to work with huge numbers like the distance between Earth and Pluto (about 3.67 billion miles!) or incredibly tiny things like the size of a single atom (around 0.00000000000001 meters). In regular writing, we sometimes use exponents (those little superscript numbers, like 10 ) to show repeated multiplication quickly. So instead of writing 10 10 10, we could just say 10 , because that means "10 times itself 3 times." In scientific notation, we take this concept a step further and include a coefficient-a smaller number that goes before the exponent. This helps us represent even larger or smaller numbers using fewer digits while still keeping track of their true value. Let me give you some examples so you can see how it works: * **Base 10:** The most familiar base is 10, and here's our earlier example written in scientific notation form: $100 \cdot \tau = 6.283 \times 10^2$. The coefficient is 6.283, and the exponent tells us how many times to multiply the base (10) by itself. In this case, $10^2$ means "10 times itself 2 times," which gives us 100. When you put them together ($6.283 \times 100$), you get the original large number. * **Base 16:** Sometimes scientists and mathematicians use other bases too, depending on what makes sense for whatever problem they're trying to solve. Let's try another example using base 16: $100 \cdot \tau = 2.745_{16} \times 10_{16}^2$. Wait a minute! What's up with those subscripted 16s next to the 2 and the 10? That just means we're working in base 16 now, also known as hexadecimal. It's kind of like having ten fingers AND ten toes - suddenly, you need extra symbols beyond just 0 through 9! Instead of inventing new characters, though, hexadecimal reuses letters A through F for its additional six symbols after 9. So in this example, 2.745 represents the same value as 6.283 decimal, and $10_{16}^2$ stands for 10 hexadecimal times itself twice, which equals 100 decimal again. So why bother with all this weird notation? Well, imagine you were playing a game where each square had a unique code made from two numbers - one horizontal and one vertical. With scientific notation, you could arrange your codes into tidy tables based on their coefficients and exponents, making it easy to find the exact square you wanted without getting lost among hundreds or thousands of possibilities. Just like organizing toys or books in groups according to type or size, scientific notation lets us keep our giant numbers neatly sorted for quick access whenever we need them! In the United States and some of English-speaking Canada, students at tax funded schools have historically been required to shower with classmates of the same sex after physical education classes. In the United States, public objections and the threat of lawsuits have resulted in a number of school districts in recent years changing policy to make showers optional. Now showering together (sometimes called communal nude showering) after sports is not common anymore at schools in the United States or Canada. Many students wear shorts in the shower. At new schools there are separate (individual) shower stalls (closets) for each student to shower in. At many older schools the showers are never used. Students go to the next class sweaty and smelly. Communal nude showering is still common at colleges, public pools, universities and health clubs. Title: How to Delete Internet Explorer History Internet Explorer is a popular web browser developed by Microsoft that allows users to access websites and online content. Over time, as you browse the internet using Internet Explorer, your browsing history gets stored locally on your device. This includes information such as the websites you've visited, files you've downloaded, cookies, and temporary internet files. While having this data can sometimes be useful, there might be instances where you want or need to delete some or all of your Internet Explorer history. To help you with this task, we have prepared an extensive, step-by-step guide below. **Key Tips and Guidelines:** * Deleting your Internet Explorer history will log you out of any sites you are currently logged into. Make sure you save any work before proceeding. * Clearing your history may also remove saved passwords and autofill form data. You may wish to note down important login credentials before following these steps. * If you share your computer with others, consider signing out of your Microsoft account before clearing your history to prevent unintended changes to your personalized settings and preferences. **Step 1: Open Internet Explorer** To begin deleting your Internet Explorer history, first launch the application by clicking on its icon either from your desktop, start menu, or taskbar. Alternatively, press `Win + R` keys simultaneously, type `iexplore` in the Run dialog box, then hit Enter. **Step 2: Access the Settings Menu** Once Internet Explorer has opened, look for the gear icon at the top right corner of the window (or upper-right corner if your layout is set to Classic). Click on this icon to open the Settings menu. From here, select 'Internet options'. ![The Gear Icon]( Then she starts saying more, and she goes don't worry I'm not going to have that much fun. Well I don't like the sauce on it. Rounding off numbers This chapter will teach us how to round off numbers. Rounding off is a method used in mathematics to decrease the quantity of numbers while keeping its value. The resulting number after rounding off is less precise however it is not difficult to use. Here are the steps in rounding off numbers: First, determine the last digit to retain. For example; tens, hundreds, tenths, hundredths, decimal places, significant numbers among others. Do not change the number if the value of the next digit or number is less than 5. This method is called rounding down. Round 43 to the nearest 10 Since 4 is in the tens position that is the digit we keep. 3 is less than 5 so we round it down to 0. Therefore, 43 is rounded to the nearest 10 is 40. However, if the value of the next digit or number is equal to or more than 5, then increase it by 1. This method is called rounding up since we increase the value of the number. Round 75 to the nearest 10 As we can see, the digit in the tens position is 7 so we keep it. The next number is equal to 5 and as mentioned above, we add 1 to the number we retain making 7 to 8. Thereby, 75 is rounded up to 80. How to round off decimals? In rounding off decimals, we determine how many numbers we retain by counting the decimal places. For example; tenths, hundredths, thousandths, and so on. - 1.235 rounded to tenths Since the next digit is 3 which is less than 5 so we retain the number 2 - 4.768 rounded to hundredths - 3.9030 rounded to thousandths - 2.65 rounded to tenths - 765.89091 rounded to ten thousandths Rounding off whole numbers Rounding off whole numbers is easier. We will still follow the same procedure above. However, when the number is less than 5 we need to change the next digit to 0. - 56 rounded to tens The number on the tens place is 5 so we keep it. But the number that follows it is greater than 5. According to the procedure above, when the digit that follows the number we retain is equal to or greater than the number 5, we add 1 to the number we keep. And so, we add 1 to 5 making it 6. - 943 rounded to hundreds - 45902 rounded to thousands - 3456 rounded to tens - 3459 rounded to thousands Rounding off to significant digits Significant digits or numbers are numbers bearing a value. This includes all numbers except leading zeros. 0.009 (This number only has 1 significant digit which is 9) The principle in rounding off is still the same as above. In rounding off significant figures, we just have to be careful with counting the significant digits. - 1.908 rounded to 3 significant digits - 2.984 rounded to 2 significant digits As you can see 0 here is a significant digit because it is a trailing zero - 0.000532 rounded to 1 significant digit - 39.045 rounded to 1 significant figure - 456.0043 rounded to 5 significant digits It looks like a pipe with a face on it. Title: How to Contact TripAdvisor: A Comprehensive Guide Introduction: TripAdvisor is one of the most popular travel websites that allows users to share reviews and opinions about hotels, restaurants, attractions, and other traveling experiences. If you have any questions or concerns regarding their services, there are several ways to reach out to them. This article will guide you through the process of contacting TripAdvisor with clear steps, helpful tips, and useful information. Step 1: Visit the official TripAdvisor website ([www.tripadvisor.com](http://www.tripadvisor.com)) and ensure you're logged into your account if necessary. Logging in may help expedite support requests related to personal accounts or reservations. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Make sure you access the correct website URL as phishing sites might try to mimic TripAdvisor. - Keep your login credentials secure by using strong passwords and enabling two-factor authentication when available. Step 2: Navigate to the Help Center page by clicking on the 'Help' link at the bottom of the homepage. The Help Center contains frequently asked questions and answers, which can save time before reaching out directly to customer service. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Use keywords relevant to your issue while searching within the Help Center; this increases the likelihood of finding an immediate solution without having to submit a request. - Bookmark the Help Center for future reference. Step 3: Scroll down the Help Center page until you see the section titled 'Still Need Help?' Underneath this header, click on the blue button labeled 'Contact Us'. You'll be prompted to provide more details about your query. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Be specific about what kind of assistance you need so that you're directed towards the appropriate department right away. Step 4: Fill out the form provided under the category corresponding to your needs. For example, select 'Manage My Account', 'Travel Planning Questions', 'Report a Violation', etc. Once selected, describe your problem thoroughly but concisely. Remember to include important information such as confirmation numbers, dates, hotel names, etc., depending on the nature of your concern. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Double-check all the fields before submitting your request. - Provide accurate information since this determines how quickly they respond and resolve your issue. Step 5: Choose whether you want to receive updates via email or SMS text messages. Then hit the green 'Submit' button to send off your request. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Opt for whichever communication method suits you best. - Note down your case number once submitted for tracking purposes. Additional Information - Phone Support: While primarily web-based, TripAdvisor does offer phone support for certain issues. However, note that wait times could be substantial during peak hours due to high demand. Here's how to get started: 1. Dial +1-781-800-5 " Are you behind the ball? Title: How to Configure GoDaddy Email on Android Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through the process of configuring your GoDaddy email account on an Android device. By following these steps, you'll be able to send and receive emails using your GoDaddy-hosted email address directly from your smartphone or tablet. Step 1: Accessing the Email Settings on Your Android Device First, unlock your Android device and open the 'Settings' app. Scroll down until you find the 'Accounts' option (it may also appear as 'Cloud and accounts') and tap on it. This section allows managing all your connected email addresses and other online services. Key tip: If you cannot locate the Accounts option within the Settings menu, try searching for it by tapping the search icon at the top of the screen. Step 2: Adding a New Account Inside the Accounts settings, look for the 'Add account' button (usually located near the bottom) and click on it. A list of common email providers should appear; however, since GoDaddy is not included, choose the 'Other' or 'Email' option instead. You can now manually configure your account details. Guideline: Make sure that the WiFi or mobile data connection is enabled before proceeding with this step. Otherwise, you won't be able to access the internet and verify your email credentials. Step 3: Inputting Your GoDaddy Email Address and Password You will now need to enter your full GoDaddy email address (e.g., [example@yourdomain.com](mailto:example@yourdomain.com)) along with its corresponding password. After inputting both pieces of information, press the 'Manual setup' button to continue setting up your account. Step 4: Choosing IMAP Protocol Next, select either POP3 or IMAP protocol to sync your GoDaddy email. For most users, we recommend choosing the IMAP protocol because it keeps your messages synchronized across multiple devices without requiring manual backups. However, if storage space is a concern, consider selecting the POP3 protocol which downloads emails onto one device only. Step 5: Configuring Server Settings Now, it's time to input server settings based on your chosen protocol type: IMAP settings: * Server: imap.secureserver.net * Security Type: SSL/TLS * Port: 993 POP3 settings: * Server: pop.secureserver.net * Security Type: SSL/TLS * Port: 995 After entering these details, ensure that the 'Require sign-in' box has been checked and then fill out the necessary fields below: * Username: Enter your full GoDaddy email address again * Password: Repeat your GoDaddy email password Step 6: Saving Your Configuration Tap the 'Next' button after completing Step 5. Your Android device will test the entered configuration. Upon successful verification, give your account a name (for example, 'GoDaddy Mail'), set up any additional options like synchronization frequency, and finally hit 'Done'. Congratulations! You have successfully configured your GoDaddy email on your Android device. Now you can start sending and receiving emails seamlessly while on the go. Keep your login information safe and remember to update your password whenever needed. [DUP takes the ball and throws it] Linda gave you the puppet for your birthday. [as In sticks toy Indian in cup] Race to Say /A/! Beginning Reading Design 1.Chart with "Amy bakes cakes all day in May." 2.A zip-lock bag for each student containing the Elkonin boxes and letters needed for the LBL lesson (a,t,e,b,k,e,g,h,u,g,w,v,e,z,p,r,i,c,d,r,o,p,l,n,t,s,c,p) 3.Elkonin boxes for modeling and letters needed to spell the word flake 4.A copy of the decodable book Jane and Babe for each student 5.Primary paper and pencils 6.Copies of attached a_e=/A/ practice worksheet for each student 7.Dry erase board and markers 8. Pseudoword note cards with the following printed on them: FAP, DAKE, DAT, FLAPE, WAT, BAGE, and HADE 1.First, review /a/=a. Then introduce a_e=/A/. Say, "Today we are going to talk about the letter a. We have already learned that a says /a/ in many words such as pat, tap, cat, and nap, but the letter a can also have a different sound. A says its name, the sound /A/, in words when it is followed by a consonant and then the letter e. When you hear this sound /A/ in words, think of somebody saying 'Hello!' or 'AAAAAA!' Let's all wave to each other and say 'AAAA!' together." (Say it together). 2."Now let's say a tongue twister that uses the /A/ sound. (Put the tongue twister on the board so that everybody can read it along together.) Amy bakes cakes all day in May. Let's say it together. (Say it together.) Now, let's make the /A/ sound in the words longer. Aaaaaaamy baaaaakes caaaaakes in all daaaaaay in Maaaaaay. Good job! 3."Now I already told you that a says its name and makes the /A/ sound in words when it is followed by a consonant and then the letter e. Let's look at some of those words on the board. How about the word cane? (Write cane on the board.) We can see that the letter a is followed by the consonant n and the letter e. Do we hear the letter e said at the end of the word? /k/ /A/ /n/ cane! No, the e is silent. The e just lets you know to say /A/ instead of /a/. Now, what if we take off the letter e? (Erase the letter e). What is that word? Can! Right! Let's try another word. (Write plan on the board.) What word is this? /p/ /l/ /a/ /n/ plan. Right! Now, if I add an e to the end, that changes how we say that word. (Add an e at the end of plan.). Now it says, /p/ /l/ /A/ /n/ plane. Let's try some more." (Repeat the two sequences with the words made/mad and glade/glad). 4. Now do the letterbox lesson for a_e=/A/. The students will already be familiar with this method of spelling and phoneme correspondence practice. The lesson will consist of some short vowel review words and words relevant to this new correspondence. Model for the students before they begin using your own letterboxes and letters. Say, "Let's practice spelling some of these words. Today we have learned that a_e=/A/. (Write this on the board). The e is silent, so it will not go in the letterboxes, but just outside the last one. Let me show you how I spell the word flake. Now to spell this word we will need four boxes. The first sound I hear is the /f/, so I will put the letter f in the first letterbox. Now this word has a lot of sounds scrunched up in the beginning so I am going to stretch it out so I can hear each sound really well. FfffffllllAAAAkkkk. The next sound I hear after the /f/ is /l/, so I will put the letter l in the next letterbox. F-l-AAAkkk. The next sound I hear is the long /A/ sound. That means I will put the letter a in the third box. Now I need the e to make the a say its name, so I will put the e outside the last letterbox. I still have not spelled the word flake yet. I still hear the /k/ sound at the end of the word, so I will put the letter k in the fourth and last letter box, just before the e. Now I want you to spell some words". (Hand out bags to each child containing all the letters they will need and the Elkonin boxes.) The following words will be used for the LBL. Say a sentence out loud that goes along with each word as you call them out for the students to spell. This way, the students will hear and understand the meanings of the words. 2 phoneme words: ate 3 phoneme words: bake, gate, hug, wave, zap 4 phoneme words: brick, drop, plate, tent 5 phoneme words: scrape Walk around while the students are working to make sure they are using the letterboxes correctly and spelling the words correctly. Give guidance as needed. Next, write all the words on the board. Call on different students to read them aloud. 5.Pass out individual copies of the decodable book Jane and Babe to each student. Say, "Now we are going to read a good book called Jane and Babe. This book has a lot of words with the /A/ sound in it. I will put you in pairs and you two will find a comfy spot in the room and take turns reading aloud to each other. Help each other if one of you is having trouble. If you both are having trouble decoding a word, raise your hand and I will help you. This story is about a zookeeper named Jane. She takes care of a big lion named Babe. She has to wake him up to feed him and clean his cage. Will Babe be mad when Jane wakes him up? Read to find out!" 6.After reading, students will go back to their desks and take out primary paper and a pencil. Say, "Now it is time for us to write a message. I want you all to tell me in one or two complete sentences what happened in the story you read, Jane and Babe. Was Babe mad when Jane woke him up? What did they do together? What does Jane do to take care of Babe? Write about anything you remembered from the story." 7.Pass out the a_e=/A/ practice worksheet. The worksheet will contain pictures of a cake, a gate, a plane, and a lake. The names of these pictures will be typed underneath them, and the students will also have to write the names of the pictures underneath each. Have students complete the worksheet after they have finished writing their message. Model how to complete the worksheet. 8.For assessment, call each student up to a desk/table individually. Show them flashcards with pseudowords written on each. Students then read the words to check for understanding of a = /a/ and a_e = /A/. Example words are: FAP, DAKE, DAT, FLAPE, WAT, BAGE, and HADE. And do not come in through the bathroom. [pitch gets very high; 1 sec; male voice in background] Lesson 2: Introduction to nouns; first declension nouns; cases of nouns What is a noun? A noun is a person, place or thing. Nouns are divided into groups called declensions. Nouns that end in 'a' belong to the first declension. They are mostly feminine. In Latin, there are no words for 'a' or 'the'. When you are reading a document, you can decide which meaning is appropriate. These are examples of Latin nouns from the first declension. Have you noticed that they all end in 'a'? People's names are also nouns. In Latin, women's names often end in 'a'. All Latin nouns have a gender - they are either masculine, feminine or neuter. Even charters and parishes have a gender! In English we give some nouns a gender, for example we describe ships as 'she'. [MOT looks at puzzle box and sees that it is recommended for children ages 3-8] Internet Explorer caches visited content in the Temporary Internet Files folder to allow quicker access (or offline access) to previously visited pages. The content is indexed in a database file, known as Index.dat. Multiple Index.dat files exist which index different content - visited content, web feeds, visited URLs, cookies, etc. Have you seen the ads for Peter Pan? Probably the most important idea before Mendel was that of Charles Darwin, whose idea of "pangenesis" had two parts. The first, that persistent hereditary units were passed on from one generation to another, was quite right. The second was his idea that they were replenished by 'gemmules' from the somatic (body) tissues. This was entirely wrong, and plays no part in science today. Darwin was right about one thing: whatever happens in evolution must happen by means of heredity, and so an accurate science of genetics is fundamental to the theory of evolution. Attracting users to comment on your forum thread on wikiHow can help increase engagement, foster a sense of community, and provide valuable feedback or insights. Here's a comprehensive guide with detailed steps and explanations: ### Step 1: Choose an Engaging Topic Select a topic that is relevant, timely, and interesting to the wikiHow community. Consider popular trends, frequently asked questions, or topics with broad appeal. An engaging title will pique curiosity and encourage more people to read and comment on your thread. This directly impacts user engagement and increases the likelihood of attracting comments. Key tip: Use keywords related to your chosen topic to make your thread easily discoverable via search functions. ### Step 2: Craft a Compelling Introduction Write a brief yet captivating introduction that summarizes the purpose of your thread. Explain why this topic matters and what value users might gain from participating in the discussion. Make sure your tone is inviting and inclusive, as this sets the stage for further interaction. Guideline: Address potential responders directly by using words like 'you,' encouraging them to share their thoughts or experiences. ### Step 3: Provide Context and Background Information Offer essential context about the issue you are addressing to ensure everyone has enough background information before diving into discussions. Including links to credible sources, previous threads, or related articles may also be helpful. By equipping participants with sufficient knowledge, they feel confident sharing their opinions. Key tip: Avoid jargon and overly technical language to maintain accessibility for all readers. ### Step 4: Ask Open-Ended Questions Prompt users to engage with specific open-ended questions throughout the body of your post. These queries should invite diverse responses and spark meaningful conversation. Remember, well thought out prompts lead to richer conversations. Guideline: Encourage different perspectives by asking questions such as "What do you think?" or "Have you had any similar experiences?" instead of yes/no questions. ### Step 5: Set Expectations Clearly Define acceptable behavior and etiquette within your thread. For instance, request constructive criticism only, no spamming, and respectful dialogue towards others. Clear expectations establish trust among participants and create a safe space for open communication. Key tip: Regularly monitor your thread and remove any inappropriate content promptly. ### Step 6: Respond Actively and Positively Engage with those who have commented on your thread by responding actively and positively. Show appreciation for their input, answer their questions honestly, and build upon their ideas where possible. Active participation encourages continued engagement from other users. Guideline: Always thank users for taking time to contribute to the discussion. It fosters goodwill and motivates others to join the conversation too. ### Step 7: Promote Your Thread Effectively Share your thread across various platforms (social media sites, blogs, etc.) after ensuring it complies with each platform's policies. More visibility leads to higher chances of drawing interested individuals into the conversation. Key tip: Leverage SEO strategies when promoting your thread online to improve its ranking in search results. Remember, creating an active and thriving discussion takes effort and patience. Stay consistent in applying these steps, keep refining your approach based on feedback received, and soon you'll see increased user engagement in your forum threads! Creating your own e-card is an exciting way to send personalized greetings to friends, family, or colleagues. By following this comprehensive guide, you'll be able to create unique and engaging e-cards that truly reflect your personality and creativity. **Step 1: Determine the Purpose and Recipient(s)** Before starting, determine the purpose of your e-card (e.g., birthday, anniversary, congratulations) and identify your recipient(s). This information will help shape your design choices and content later on. **Step 2: Choose a Platform or Tool** Select a platform or tool to create your e-card. There are various free and paid options available online such as Adobe Spark, Canva, Greeting Card Universe, Smilebox, and PicMonkey. These platforms typically offer pre-designed templates, customization tools, and sharing capabilities via email or social media. Research their features and choose one that fits your needs and skill level. **Key Tips:** * Consider compatibility with different devices if you plan to collaborate with others. * Opt for user-friendly interfaces to minimize frustration during the creation process. * Check storage limits and pricing plans before committing to a particular service. **Step 3: Select a Template or Start from Scratch** Decide whether you want to start with a pre-made template or build your design from scratch. Templates can save time but may limit originality; designing from scratch allows more creative freedom at the cost of additional effort. Balance these factors when making your decision. **Step 4: Customize Design Elements** Personalize your e-card by modifying visual elements like backgrounds, colors, fonts, shapes, and clipart. Make sure they align with the occasion and recipient preferences. Keep text minimal yet impactful, focusing on clear messaging over lengthy paragraphs. **Key Guidelines:** * Maintain consistency in color schemes and typography throughout the card. * Use high-quality images relevant to the theme and message. * Optimize layout for readability across multiple screen sizes. **Step 5: Add Personal Touch through Text Content** Craft meaningful messages tailored to the recipient(s). Avoid generic phrases and opt for sincere expressions instead. If humor suits the relationship, add subtle jokes or witty remarks to make the card memorable. Don't forget essential details such as names, dates, places, etc. **Step 6: Preview and Test Functionality** Preview your e-card to ensure all components display correctly. Double-check spelling, grammar, and alignment errors. Send test emails to yourself or trusted contacts to verify delivery and viewing experience. Address any issues before finalizing the design. **Step 7: Save and Export** Save your e-card in a suitable format compatible with your chosen distribution method. Most platforms support popular file types like JPEG, PNG, PDF, or MP4. Download copies for safekeeping and future reference. **Step 8: Distribute Your E-Card** Share your e-card with recipients using email, social media, or cloud services like Google Drive or Dropbox. Provide necessary context and warm wishes along with the link or attachment. Monitor engagement metrics (if available) to gauge reception and adjust future creations accordingly. By following this thorough tutorial, you now possess the skills needed to craft impressive e-cards that leave lasting impressions. Happy creating! Emergent Literacy Design By Leah Steiner order for children to learn to read and spell words, they need the insight that letters stand for phonemes and that spellings map out the in spoken words. Before children can match letters to phonemes, to recognize the phonemes. This lesson will help children to = /A/. They will learn to recognize a_e = /A/ in words by learning a meaningful representation and the letter symbol, practice applying a_e = /A/. "James and the Good Day," Carson CA: Educational Insights, 1990. Snake and Plane with a picture of a snake or plane and a_e = /A/ on them. various pictures of /A/ sounding objects: egg, face, bed, cake, wave, plane, bat, snake, gate, crab, lake, grass, skate, rake, plate. 1. Introduce the lesson by explaining that made up of individual sounds. "Each word is made up of many sounds and when we the different sounds they make different words. Today we are going to work on sounding out words with a_e and making words - Tell students: "Think about going to a baseball game and imagine different things that go on at the game. Think again about the /A/ sound that we are learning about. Can anyone think of words to describe baseball that make the /A/ sound?" (Ex. Players chase the ball, there is a first, second and third base, it is called a game, players on the same team work together, your jersey has your name on the back, sometimes players pretend to throw the ball and this is called a fake, they rake the red clay before every game, sometimes players are late to the game, you have team mates, sometimes you have to be brave when the ball pops up). These are all great examples of the /A/ sound. Let's pretend we are throwing a baseball and while we throw our baseball, we are going to drag it out and say bAAAseball. Great Job!!! - "Let's try a tongue twister. "Jake hates to race because he makes a funny face. Now let's try it again and stretch out the /A/ sound. JAAAke hAAAtes to rAAAce because he mAAAkes a funny fAAAce. Good job. One more time!" - "Now take out a piece of primary paper and your pencil. Let's practice writing lowercase a and e. (On the board write an a, then go through the following steps: for lowercase a, you start under the fence then you go around and touch the sidewalk, around and straight down. Then write an e, while explaining the following steps to make an e: for lowercase e get in the center of the space below the fence, go toward the door (right), up to touch the fence around and up. I want to see everyone's a and e. After I place a snake or plane sticker on it, make a row of a's and a row of e's just like the first one." - "We are going to listen for /A/ in some words that I am about to say." (Pass out index cards with snake and plane pictures on them.) "When I say a word and you hear /A/ I want everyone to hold up their snake or plane card. If you don't hear /A/ then be sure to keep your cards down." Use the words lace, cab, get, apple, cake, sad, trade, hot, skate, jaw, sit, grate. - Read " James and the Good Day." Tell the students to hold up their snake or plane card every time a word with the /A / sound is read. - For assessment, distribute the picture page and help students name each picture. Students will then circle the pictures with the a_e = /A/ in their Eldridge, J. Lloyd (1995). "Teaching Decoding in Holistic Classrooms." New Jersey: Merrill, 1995, pp.50-70. Creating a virtual desktop is an excellent way to organize your applications and windows on your computer, especially if you are working with multiple programs at once or have a cluttered screen. A virtual desktop allows you to create separate workspaces where you can group related tasks together, making it easier to manage and switch between them as needed. This tutorial will guide you through creating a virtual desktop using Windows 10 as an example operating system. **Step 1: Open Task View** The first step in creating a virtual desktop is opening the Task View feature built into Windows 10. You can do this by clicking on the Task View button located on the taskbar near the Start menu (it looks like two overlapping rectangles). Alternatively, press the `Windows` + `Tab` keys simultaneously on your keyboard. Key tip: If you don't see the Task View button on your taskbar, right-click any empty space on the taskbar and select "Show Task View button" from the contextual menu that appears. **Step 2: Create a New Desktop** Once the Task View window has opened, look towards the bottom-right corner. Here, you should find a "+New deskop" option. Click on this to create your new virtual desktop. Guideline: Name your desktops appropriately so you know which one contains specific applications or projects when switching back later. **Step 3: Move Applications Between Desktops** To move existing open application windows to your newly created desktop, click and drag the window thumbnail(s) found within the Task View interface onto the desired desktop preview. Release the mouse button when hovered over the target desktop; the application will then appear there instead of the original workspace. Tip: To quickly transfer all running applications from one desktop to another, right-click a desktop preview and choose "Move all to..." followed by selecting the destination desktop. **Step 4: Switch Between Desktops** Now that you have set up your virtual desktops and organized your applications accordingly, learn how to navigate between these spaces efficiently. Use either of these methods: * Click directly on the desired desktop preview within the Task View interface. * Press `Ctrl` + `Win` + `Right Arrow` / `Left Arrow` shortcut keys to cycle through the available desktops sequentially. **Step 5: Close Unwanted Desktops** If you no longer require certain desktops, close them down to declutter your list of options. Access the Task View interface again, then hover your cursor over the unwanted desktop preview. An 'X' icon should appear in its top-right corner - click this to remove the unused desktop. Note: Closing a desktop does _not_ affect any applications still residing on other active desktops. By following this comprehensive tutorial, you now possess the knowledge required to harness the power of virtual desktops effectively. Organize your digital workspace with ease, increase productivity, and maintain focus while managing multiple tasks concurrently! If we put on the Nintendo he'd talk plenty. Lovely stew with dumplings on top husband dear. X-ray is a series of pictures of the bones of the skull. Skull X-rays have largely been replaced by computed tomography (CT) scans. are a form of radiation, like light or radio waves, that are focused into a beam, much like a flashlight beam. X-rays can pass through most objects, including the human body. X-rays make a picture by striking a detector that either exposes a film or sends the picture to a computer. Dense tissues in the body, such as bones, block (absorb) many of the X-rays and look white on an X-ray picture. Less dense tissues, such as muscles and organs, block fewer of the X-rays (more of the X-rays pass through) and look like shades of gray on an X-ray. X-rays that pass only through air look black on the picture. A skull X-ray may help find head injuries, bone fractures, or abnormal growths or changes in bone structure or size. [I can see if you have enough money to buy one by looking in your wallet, and then I'll tell you whether you have enough.] Rationale: This lesson is intended to help students recognize /sh/. Students need to become aware that digraphs are groups of two successive letters whose phonemic value is a single sound. This lesson will also help students when reading and writing words with /sh/. The lesson is aimed at helping students better understand the digraph /sh/. I will teach this correspondence by using a letterbox lesson. Rainbow Fish by Marcus Phister. There should be enough boxes,letters,and books for each child,chalk and chalkboard. 1. Have the students place their hand in front of their mouth while making the /sh/ sound. Do you feel air on your hand? The /sh/ sound is made by putting your teeth together and blowing out of your mouth. 2. Say: I am going to read a sentence that has the /sh/ in it more than one time. Each time you hear a word with the /sh/ sound,clap so I know that you heard it. Try to count how many times you clapped so we can figure out how many times I said /sh/. "Sharon sold sea shells and fish by the sea shore." 3. Then ask the students what letters they think makes the They reply. Write the words the class told you on the board. Discuss words such as wish,fish,and ship. Model the sound and give the following explanation. The/sh/ sound is made when you put the letters s and h together. The /sh/ sound is found in many words,lets spell some of 4. Start the letterbox lesson,be sure every child has the and letter boxes. Say: first spell she,Great,now let's spell fish, Good, now let's spell shell,O.K. let's try the word ship. Great you can spell the word ship. Excellent ,now spell cash. Open up four letter boxes and try to spell the word trash. 5. Now write each of the words on the board and have the 6. Say: Everyone has just spelled six words that have /sh/ sound in them. All the words that we just spelled are in this book that two are about to read. Say: The Rainbow Fish by Marcus Phister (book talk),is about a fish who learns to share with other fish. While we are reading listen for the /sh/ sounds. 7. Assessment: Ask the students if they remember any book that made the /sh/ sound. Write them on the board. When the list is complete,point to each word and ask the students, one at a time to read them back to you. Accessing your U-verse router is an important task if you want to customize your network settings or monitor connected devices. This guide will walk you through the process step-by-step, providing detailed explanations along the way. **Step 1: Gather Information about Your Network** Before accessing your U-verse router, gather information about your network. You'll need to know: * The IP address of your router (usually 192.168.1.254) * Your U-verse account username and password To find your router's IP address: 1. Press Windows Key + X and select 'Command Prompt' or 'PowerShell'. 2. Type `ipconfig` and press Enter. 3. Look for the line that says 'Default Gateway'; this shows your router's IP address. Key tip: Keep your U-verse account details handy as they are required later in the process. --- **Step 2: Connect to Your Wireless Network** Ensure that your device is connected to your U-verse Wi-Fi network before proceeding. If you don't remember your wireless network name (SSID) or password, check the label at the bottom or back of your router. --- **Step 3: Open a Web Browser** Open a web browser like Google Chrome, Mozilla Firefox, Microsoft Edge, or Safari. It doesn't matter which one you choose; just make sure it's up-to-date. --- **Step 4: Enter Your Router's IP Address** In the URL bar, type your router's IP address (found in Step 1), then hit Enter. You should see a login page asking for a username and password. --- **Step 5: Log into Your Router** Enter your router's default credentials to log in. For most U-verse routers, the default username and password are: * Username: admin * Password: password If these don't work, consult the user manual or look for a sticker on the router itself. Once logged in, change the default credentials to secure your router. --- **Step 6: Customize Settings and Monitor Devices** After logging in, explore the different tabs and options available within your router interface. Here are some common tasks: * **Change Wi-Fi Name and Password:** Go to 'Wireless Setup', locate the SSID field, and enter your new network name. Then set a strong password under 'Security Options'. Save changes when done. * **Port Forwarding:** Navigate to 'Firewall > Port Forwarding'. Add rules according to specific applications or games requiring port forwarding. Consult their documentation for necessary ports. * **Device List:** Check out the list of currently connected devices by navigating to 'Connected Devices' or similar option. Identify each device based on its IP address, MAC address, and hostname. Guideline: Remember always to save any changes made in your router settings before moving on to another section or logging out. --- By following this tutorial, you can now confidently access your U-verse router, customize network settings, and monitor connected devices. Always ensure to keep your router secured with updated firmware and complex credentials. yeah, and I bet they have no bug spray either, I didn't even think of that, I bet they forgot their bug spray my friend. Title: How to Clean a Keurig Needle: A Comprehensive Guide Introduction: A clean Keurig needle ensures that your coffee maker functions optimally by delivering water properly through the system, ultimately leading to better-tasting beverages. Over time, oils from coffee build up within the machine's internal components, including the needle, which can negatively impact performance and flavor if left unattended. This guide will walk you through the process of cleaning your Keurig needle safely and effectively. Materials Required: 1. A paperclip or specialized Keurig needle cleaning tool 2. Water (warm) 3. Mild dish soap 4. Dry cloth or towel 5. Small bowl 6. Gloves (optional) 7. Safety glasses (optional) Step 1: Unplug Your Keurig Machine For safety reasons, always start by disconnecting your Keurig from its power source before attempting any maintenance procedures. This reduces the risk of electrical shock during cleanup. Step 2: Locate the Exit Needle The exit needle is typically found at the bottom of the K-Cup holder inside the brewer head. It might look like a small piece of metal protruding slightly from the baseplate. If unsure about where to find it, refer to your user manual. Step 3: Prepare Your Cleaning Tool Straighten out a regular-sized paper clip or utilize a specialized Keurig needle cleaning tool if available. Make sure there are no sharp edges as they could potentially damage the device. Step 4: Submerge the Paper Clip in Soapy Water Mix some mild dish soap with warm water in a small bowl until suds form. Immerse the cleaned end of your paper clip into this solution for a few seconds to remove any dust particles and soften hardened coffee residue. Step 5: Insert the Cleaning Tool Into the Exit Needle With the Keurig turned off, gently insert the dampened end of your paper clip into the exit needle opening. Apply slight pressure while moving the tool back and forth several times to loosen and extract built-up debris. Be careful not to force the tool deeper than necessary; doing so may cause harm to the mechanism. Step 6: Rinse the Area Thoroughly Remove the paper clip and rinse away the loosened particles using running water. Repeat steps 4-6 if needed until all visible residues have been eliminated. Step 7: Clean the Entrance Needle (Optional but Recommended) If your model allows access to the entrance needle (usually located above the K-Cup holder), repeat the same procedure described above to ensure thorough cleaning. Remember to exercise caution when handling needles to prevent injury. Step 8: Dry All Parts Pat dry both needles and surrounding areas with a soft cloth or towel. Allow ample time for everything to air dry completely before reassembling the machine. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Always turn off and unplug your Keurig before starting the cleaning process. * Use only mild dish soap; harsh chemicals can corrode internal parts. * Never attempt to clean the needle with sharp objects; usage of pointed tools increases the chance of damaging delicate components. * Regularly scheduled cleanings help maintain optimal functionality and taste quality over time. * Although optional, consider wearing gloves and protective eyewear to minimize direct contact with wet surfaces and potential splashes.
